strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and presumably no shared test coverage. The 8K-line codebase is poorly tested, making this a high-risk refactor without a clear seam. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation in api/routes.py checks only the key itself, not the associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still work. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. The fix must not conflate human users and service accounts. The security fix has been applied and works. New performance concern: the fix adds 2 extra DB queries per API request, unacceptable on high-traffic endpoints.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed but adds 2 DB queries per request
g.user caching is not the bug — Flask's g is request-scoped and always invalidated at request end

strands.title: API Key Performance — Query Reduction and Caching
strands.content:
The security fix introduced 2 queries per API request (key lookup + user status check). Three layers of optimization are available, in order of preference:

(1) JOIN FIRST: Collapse the two queries into one joined query in resolve_api_key — db.session.query(ApiKey, User).join(User).filter(...User.is_active==True...). The DB does a PK lookup on ApiKey + FK join to User, both indexed. Expected: ~0.3–0.5ms. This should be done regardless. Measure before adding a cache.

(2) SHORT TTL CACHE (Option A): Cache the (user_id, api_key_id) tuple keyed by key_hash in Redis/Flask-Caching with a 30s TTL. On cache hit, reload ORM objects from DB by PK (session.get — single PK lookup, L1 cache friendly) and re-check is_active + revoked in Python as a safety net for stale cache. Cache miss falls through to the join query. Invalidation semantics: disabled users' keys remain valid for up to TTL seconds — this is a deliberate, documented tradeoff.

(3) EXPLICIT INVALIDATION (Option B): When admin disables a user, fetch their key_hashes from DB and bust cache entries explicitly. The admin disable path is never hot so extra queries there are acceptable. Required if compliance mandates immediate revocation effect.

(4) ASYNC last_used_at WRITE: Synchronous last_used_at write on every request is likely doing more damage than the user query. Fire-and-forget via Thread or (better) push key_id to Redis list and flush in bulk every 60s with a background worker. This is explicitly best-effort — acceptable to lose under load.

CRITICAL: the TTL value and its security implications must be documented at the call site, not in a README. The comment must state: what the window is, that it's deliberate, and how to get immediate revocation (call invalidate_user_key_cache from the admin action).

key_claims.text: Join query eliminates one DB query with no semantic tradeoff — do this unconditionally
30-second TTL cache is the right default — short enough to be defensible, long enough to matter
Cache stores (user_id, api_key_id) not ORM objects — ORM objects are not safely serializable across requests
On cache hit, Python re-check of is_active + revoked is a safety net against stale cache
Explicit invalidation on admin disable is required only for immediate-revocation compliance requirements
Synchronous last_used_at write is likely the larger performance problem — async/bulk flush is the fix
TTL semantics must be documented at the call site — not README — with explicit revocation escape hatch

strands.title: Security Bug Fixes — Immediate Action
strands.content: Both security bugs should be fixed now, surgically, before the refactor. The API key bug fix has a critical constraint: service accounts share the key resolution path and must not be broken by an is_active check. The fix strategy is to branch on key type at the point of user resolution — only human user keys get the is_active check; service account keys skip it by design (and this is explicitly documented in code). Three likely schema patterns determine the exact implementation: (1) separate ApiKey and ServiceAccountToken tables with different resolution paths — cleanest; (2) ApiKey with nullable user_id and service_account_id — branch on which FK is set; (3) ApiKey with a key_type discriminator column — branch on the column value. In all cases, the fix returns a (principal, api_key) tuple rather than just ApiKey — consistent with the planned AuthService interface and allows the decorator to update last_used_at. The fix is confirmed working. Performance is the current concern.

key_claims.text: Fix is applied and confirmed working
Service account keys must bypass is_active check — service accounts have no disabled flag
Return (principal, api_key) tuple — consistent with AuthService.resolve_api_key design
Write a regression test that service account keys still work — as important as the bug fix test

raw_output.response:
Good timing to ask this — OAuth2 fits into the existing design more cleanly than you might expect. The core skeleton doesn't need to be redesigned; it needs to be *extended*. Here's my thinking:

---

## The key insight: OAuth2 is identity *acquisition*, not identity *resolution*

The `AuthService` skeleton we designed handles **identity resolution** — given a credential (session, API key, token), who is the principal? OAuth2 is different: it's how you *acquire* a verified identity from a third party and translate it into a local principal.

So the question is: where does the OAuth2 flow live, and how does the result connect to what we already designed?

---

## What OAuth2 actually needs in your architecture

**1. Provider configuration** — client IDs, secrets, scopes, redirect URIs per provider. This is config, not service logic.

**2. The OAuth flow itself** — redirect to provider, handle callback, exchange code for token, fetch user info. This is thin glue code; don't write it yourself. Use [Authlib](https://docs.authlib.org/en/latest/integrations/flask.html) or [Flask-Dance](https://flask-dance.readthedocs.io/). I'd pick **Authlib** — it's more explicit and production-grade.

**3. Account linking** — given a verified external identity (`google:1234567890`), find or create a local `User`. This is the meaty part.

**4. Session creation** — once you have a local `User`, log them in exactly the same way as a password-based login.

Steps 3 and 4 belong in `AuthService`. Steps 1 and 2 belong in a new `auth/oauth.py` routes module.

---

## Concrete changes to `auth/service.py`

Add one new method group — **OAuth Account Management**:

```python
# auth/service.py

class AuthService:
    # ... existing methods unchanged ...

    # ── OAuth Account Management ──────────────────────────────────────────

    def find_or_create_oauth_user(
        self,
        provider: str,           # "google" | "github"
        provider_user_id: str,   # the stable ID from the provider (NOT email)
        email: str,
        display_name: str | None = None,
        avatar_url: str | None = None,
    ) -> tuple[User, bool]:      # (user, created)
        """
        Given a verified OAuth identity, return the local User.
        
        Lookup order:
          1. OAuthIdentity with matching (provider, provider_user_id) → return linked user
          2. User with matching email → link this provider to existing account, return user
          3. No match → create new User + OAuthIdentity, return new user

        'created' is True only if a net-new User row was made. Use it to
        trigger onboarding flow in the caller.
        
        IMPORTANT: provider_user_id is the stable numeric/opaque ID from the
        provider — NOT the email. Emails can change; provider IDs don't.
        """
        ...

    def link_oauth_identity(
        self,
        user: User,
        provider: str,
        provider_user_id: str,
    ) -> "OAuthIdentity":
        """
        Explicitly link an OAuth provider to an existing authenticated user.
        Used in account settings when a logged-in user connects a new provider.
        """
        ...

    def unlink_oauth_identity(self, user: User, provider: str) -> None:
        """
        Remove an OAuth provider link. Guards: user must have either a
        password set OR another linked provider, or they'd be locked out.
        """
        ...
```

---

## The new model: `OAuthIdentity`

You need one new table. Add it to `auth/models.py`:

```python
# auth/models.py

class OAuthIdentity(db.Model):
    __tablename__ = "oauth_identities"
    __table_args__ = (
        db.UniqueConstraint("provider", "provider_user_id", name="uq_oauth_provider_uid"),
    )

    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    user_id = db.Column(db.Integer, db.ForeignKey("users.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False)
    provider = db.Column(db.String(32), nullable=False)          # "google", "github"
    provider_user_id = db.Column(db.String(256), nullable=False) # stable ID from provider
    email_at_link_time = db.Column(db.String(256))               # audit only, don't use for lookup
    created_at = db.Column(db.DateTime, default=datetime.utcnow)

    user = db.relationship("User", back_populates="oauth_identities")
```

Two things worth calling out:
- The unique constraint is on `(provider, provider_user_id)`, not on email. Email can change at the provider.
- `email_at_link_time` is stored for audit/support purposes only. Never use it as a lookup key.

You'll also need to update `User`:
```python
# Add to User model
oauth_identities = db.relationship("OAuthIdentity", back_populates="user", cascade="all, delete-orphan")
has_password = db.Column(db.Boolean, default=True)  # False for pure-OAuth users
```

`has_password=False` matters for `unlink_oauth_identity` — if someone signed up via Google only, they have no password, so unlinking Google locks them out.

---

## The OAuth routes: `auth/oauth.py`

This is thin glue — keep business logic out of it:

```python
# auth/oauth.py
from authlib.integrations.flask_client import OAuth
from flask import Blueprint, redirect, url_for, session, current_app
from .exceptions import AuthError

oauth_bp = Blueprint("oauth", __name__)
oauth = OAuth()

def init_oauth(app):
    oauth.init_app(app)
    oauth.register(
        name="google",
        server_metadata_url="https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration",
        client_kwargs={"scope": "openid email profile"},
    )
    oauth.register(
        name="github",
        access_token_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token",
        authorize_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize",
        api_base_url="https://api.github.com/",
        client_kwargs={"scope": "user:email"},
    )

@oauth_bp.route("/login/<provider>")
def login(provider):
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.callback", provider=provider, _external=True)
    return oauth.create_client(provider).authorize_redirect(redirect_uri)

@oauth_bp.route("/callback/<provider>")
def callback(provider):
    client = oauth.create_client(provider)
    token = client.authorize_access_token()
    
    # Normalize provider user info — each provider has different shapes
    user_info = _get_user_info(provider, client, token)
    
    auth_service = current_app.auth  # existing wiring
    user, created = auth_service.find_or_create_oauth_user(
        provider=provider,
        provider_user_id=user_info["id"],
        email=user_info["email"],
        display_name=user_info.get("name"),
    )
    
    # Reuse your existing session creation — same path as password login
    session["user_id"] = user.id
    
    if created:
        return redirect(url_for("onboarding.welcome"))
    return redirect(url_for("dashboard.index"))

def _get_user_info(provider, client, token) -> dict:
    """Normalize provider-specific user info shapes into a common dict."""
    if provider == "google":
        info = token.get("userinfo") or client.get("userinfo").json()
        return {"id": info["sub"], "email": info["email"], "name": info.get("name")}
    elif provider == "github":
        resp = client.get("user", token=token)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        # GitHub may not return email in /user — fetch separately
        email = data.get("email") or _get_github_primary_email(client, token)
        return {"id": str(data["id"]), "email": email, "name": data.get("name")}
    raise AuthError(f"Unknown OAuth provider: {provider}")

def _get_github_primary_email(client, token) -> str:
    resp = client.get("user/emails", token=token)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    emails = resp.json()
    primary = next((e["email"] for e in emails if e["primary"] and e["verified"]), None)
    if not primary:
        raise AuthError("GitHub account has no verified primary email")
    return primary
```

---

## Does the existing design need to change?

**No redesign needed.** The existing `resolve_session`, `resolve_api_key`, `resolve_service_token` are untouched. OAuth2 adds a *new concern* (account linking) but doesn't replace any existing concern. Once `find_or_create_oauth_user` runs and sets `session["user_id"]`, the existing `resolve_session` takes it from there — exactly as before.

The only thing to watch: if you currently assume `User.password_hash` is always set, you need to handle `has_password=False` users in `verify_password` and in any "change password" flows.

---

## Updated `auth/` package structure

```
auth/
├── __init__.py          # public API — unchanged
├── models.py            # + OAuthIdentity model, has_password on User
├── hashing.py           # unchanged
├── tokens.py            # unchanged
├── session.py           # unchanged
├── decorators.py        # unchanged
├── exceptions.py        # + OAuthError(AuthError) if you want provider-specific errors
├── oauth.py             # NEW — routes + provider normalization
└── service.py           # + find_or_create_oauth_user, link/unlink methods
```

---

## The edge cases that will bite you

1. **Email collision on signup**: User signs up with email `alice@example.com` via password, then later tries to log in via Google with the same email. Your `find_or_create_oauth_user` lookup-by-email path handles this — it links the provider to the existing account silently. Some teams prefer to require the user to explicitly confirm this link. Your call, but have an opinion before you ship.

2. **Email changed at provider**: Alice changes her Google email. Next OAuth login, `provider_user_id` still matches, so she gets her account. `email_at_link_time` diverges from her current email. Don't auto-update `User.email` from OAuth without confirmation — it's a potential account takeover vector.

3. **GitHub email privacy**: GitHub users can hide their email. The `_get_github_primary_email` fallback handles this, but you should also decide: is a verified email required? I'd say yes, and raise `AuthError` if none is available.

4. **CSRF on the callback**: Authlib handles the `state` parameter automatically, but make sure you're not stripping it in any middleware.

---

## Where this fits in the implementation plan

This becomes step 0c — parallel to the performance work, before the full refactor:

- `0b` (in progress): join query + cache for API key performance
- `0c` (new): `OAuthIdentity` migration + `find_or_create_oauth_user` + `auth/oauth.py` routes
- Steps 1–8: unchanged

The OAuth work is largely independent of the performance work and can go in a separate PR.

What's your current session management — Flask-Login or custom? That affects how `session["user_id"]` gets set in the callback.

The 8K-line codebase is poorly tested, making this a high-risk refactor without a clear seam. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation in api/routes.py checks only the key itself, not the associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still work. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. The fix must not conflate human users and service accounts. The security fix has been applied and works. New performance concern: the fix adds 2 extra DB queries per API request, unacceptable on high-traffic endpoints. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed but adds 2 DB queries per request
g.user caching is not the bug — Flask's g is request-scoped and always invalidated at request end
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a new product requirement added this cycle

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content: Recommended: a single auth/ package. Structure: __init__.py (public API), models.py (User + credential models + OAuthIdentity), hashing.py (password hashing utils), tokens.py (API key + service account token validation), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three auth decorators), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy + OAuthError subclass), service.py (central AuthService class), oauth.py (NEW — OAuth routes + provider normalization). The auth/service.py module is the gravitational center for identity resolution. The caching layer belongs in AuthService.resolve_api_key. OAuth2 adds a new concern (account linking / identity acquisition) but does not replace or redesign any existing concern — it extends service.py with a new method group and adds oauth.py for routes.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
AuthService must have distinct resolve paths for user API keys and service account tokens
Wire AuthService once in app factory, access via current_app.auth
Cache lives in AuthService.resolve_api_key — not in the decorator — for testability and encapsulation
OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not identity resolution — it feeds into existing session path once complete
auth/oauth.py is thin glue — business logic belongs in AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user

strands.title: AuthService Interface
strands.content: auth/service.py skeleton. Public method groups: (1) Identity Resolution — resolve_session(user_id) -> User, resolve_api_key(raw_key) -> tuple[User, ApiKey], resolve_service_token(raw_token) -> ServiceAccount. (2) Credential Management — create_api_key, revoke_api_key, rotate_service_token. (3) Password Operations — verify_password, set_password. (4) Cache Management — invalidate_user_key_cache(user_id). (5) OAuth Account Management (NEW) — find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) -> tuple[User, bool]; link_oauth_identity(user, provider, provider_user_id) -> OAuthIdentity; unlink_oauth_identity(user, provider) -> None. resolve_api_key always checks is_active; resolve_service_token never does. find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: (1) OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id); (2) User by email → link silently; (3) create new User + OAuthIdentity. unlink_oauth_identity must guard against lockout: user must have has_password=True OR another linked provider.

key_claims.text: resolve_api_key returns (User, ApiKey) tuple — decorator needs both to update last_used_at
resolve_service_token is a distinct method — service accounts bypass is_active check by design
invalidate_user_key_cache is a first-class method — called from admin actions, not just internally
find_or_create_oauth_user uses provider_user_id (stable) not email as primary lookup key
unlink_oauth_identity must check has_password or other linked providers before removing
create_api_key raw key returned once and never stored

strands.title: OAuth2 Integration
strands.content: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) fits into the existing auth/service.py design as a new method group — no redesign needed. The pattern: OAuth flow (in auth/oauth.py) acquires a verified external identity → AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user() links it to a local User → existing resolve_session() takes it from there. Library choice: Authlib (preferred over Flask-Dance — more explicit, production-grade). Provider normalization lives in auth/oauth.py._get_user_info() — each provider has different response shapes. New model: OAuthIdentity (auth/models.py) with unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id). New User field: has_password (boolean, False for pure-OAuth signups). Email collision handling: if a User with the same email exists, link the provider silently — teams may prefer explicit confirmation; this is a deliberate policy choice. Key edge cases: (1) email changed at provider — don't auto-update User.email, potential account takeover vector; (2) GitHub email privacy — must call /user/emails endpoint for verified primary email; (3) CSRF — Authlib handles state param automatically; (4) lockout guard on unlink. Email is stored as email_at_link_time for audit only — never used as a lookup key after initial account matching.

key_claims.text: OAuth2 is identity acquisition not identity resolution — existing resolve_session path is reused after OAuth completes
OAuthIdentity unique constraint is on (provider, provider_user_id) — not on email
has_password=False is required on User to support pure-OAuth signups and lockout guard
Authlib is preferred over Flask-Dance for production use
Auto-updating User.email from OAuth provider is a potential account takeover vector — don't do it
GitHub requires separate /user/emails call for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable
Silent email-based account linking on first OAuth login is the default — explicit confirmation is an optional stricter policy

strands.title: API Key Performance — Query Reduction and Caching
strands.content: The security fix introduced 2 queries per API request (key lookup + user status check). Three layers of optimization are available, in order of preference: (1) JOIN FIRST: Collapse the two queries into one joined query in resolve_api_key. Expected: ~0.3–0.5ms. Do this regardless. Measure before adding a cache. (2) SHORT TTL CACHE (Option A): Cache the (user_id, api_key_id) tuple keyed by key_hash in Redis/Flask-Caching with a 30s TTL. On cache hit, reload ORM objects by PK and re-check is_active + revoked in Python as safety net. (3) EXPLICIT INVALIDATION (Option B): When admin disables a user, bust cache entries explicitly. Required if compliance mandates immediate revocation. (4) ASYNC last_used_at WRITE: Synchronous last_used_at write is likely doing more damage than the user query. Fire-and-forget or Redis list + bulk flush every 60s. CRITICAL: TTL value and security implications must be documented at the call site.

key_claims.text: Join query eliminates one DB query with no semantic tradeoff — do this unconditionally
30-second TTL cache is the right default — short enough to be defensible, long enough to matter
Cache stores (user_id, api_key_id) not ORM objects — ORM objects are not safely serializable across requests
Synchronous last_used_at write is likely the larger performance problem — async/bulk flush is the fix
TTL semantics must be documented at the call site — not README — with explicit revocation escape hatch

strands.title: Security Bug Fixes — Immediate Action
strands.content: Both security bugs should be fixed now, surgically, before the refactor. The API key bug fix has a critical constraint: service accounts share the key resolution path and must not be broken by an is_active check. Fix strategy: branch on key type at point of user resolution — only human user keys get the is_active check; service account keys skip it by design. Three likely schema patterns determine the exact implementation: (1) separate tables; (2) nullable user_id and service_account_id; (3) key_type discriminator. In all cases, the fix returns a (principal, api_key) tuple. The fix is confirmed working. Performance is the current parallel concern.

key_claims.text: Fix is applied and confirmed working
Service account keys must bypass is_active check — service accounts have no disabled flag
Write a regression test that service account keys still work — as important as the bug fix test

strands.title: Implementation Plan
strands.content: Ordered migration steps: (0) DONE: security patches applied and confirmed working. (0b) IN PROGRESS: optimize resolve_api_key with join query; add cache if profiling confirms need; make last_used_at write async. (0c) NEW: OAuthIdentity migration + find_or_create_oauth_user + auth/oauth.py routes. Steps 0b and 0c are independent and can be separate PRs. (1) auth/exceptions.py — unblocks everything; (2) auth/models.py (includes OAuthIdentity, has_password on User); (3) auth/service.py with tests including cache invalidation and OAuth account linking; (4) auth/decorators.py; (5) auth/hashing.py; (6) auth/session.py; (7) auth/oauth.py; (8) auth/__init__.py; (9) cleanup call sites. The OAuth work is largely independent of the performance work.

key_claims.text: Security patches are done — performance and OAuth are current parallel active work
Steps 0b (performance) and 0c (OAuth) are independent — separate PRs
exceptions.py must come first in the full refactor — it unblocks all other extractions
OAuth routes (auth/oauth.py) come after service.py is extracted — they depend on it

depends_on: API Key Performance — Query Reduction and Caching

strands.title: Unknown Unknowns
strands.content: Remaining unknowns: (1) Service account schema — same table vs separate, discriminator vs nullable FK. (2) Whether Redis/Flask-Caching is already in the stack — determines cache backend. (3) What the current last_used_at write pattern looks like. (4) Whether there are compliance/audit requirements around immediate revocation. (5) Flask-Login vs custom session management — this is now more pressing with OAuth: Authlib's callback sets session directly; if Flask-Login is in use, need login_user(user) instead of session['user_id'] = user.id. (6) Email collision policy preference — silent link vs explicit confirmation on first OAuth login.

key_claims.text: Whether Redis is in the stack determines cache backend choice
Flask-Login vs custom sessions is now more urgent with OAuth — affects how the callback logs the user in
Email collision policy (silent link vs explicit confirmation) is a product decision, not a technical one

open_questions: Is Redis already in the stack? Determines cache backend for API key performance.
Do you have compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation on user disable? Determines whether explicit cache invalidation is required.
What does the current last_used_at write look like — synchronous per-request?
Is Flask-Login in use or custom session management? Now urgent — affects how the OAuth callback logs the user in (login_user() vs session['user_id'] = ...).
Email collision policy: when an OAuth user's email matches an existing password-based account, do you want silent linking or explicit user confirmation?
What is the ApiKey table schema? (discriminator column, nullable FKs, or separate tables for service accounts)
raw_output.instructions_for_next: User may: (a) answer the Flask-Login question — highest priority now given OAuth; (b) clarify email collision policy; (c) ask about the OAuthIdentity migration specifics (Alembic); (d) ask about testing the OAuth flow (mocking provider responses); (e) ask about account settings UI (link/unlink providers); (f) push back on Authlib vs Flask-Dance. Key risk: if Flask-Login is in use, the oauth.py callback needs login_user(user) not raw session manipulation — and Flask-Login's remember_me and session protection features interact with OAuth callbacks in non-obvious ways. Also watch for: if they have existing users with no has_password column, the migration needs a default=True backfill.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Explicit detail that 'create_api_key raw key returned once and never stored' was listed as a standalone key claim in AuthService Interface
declared_losses.why: Still present in the strand content implicitly but dropped from key_claims to make room for higher-priority OAuth-related claims. Low information loss — this is a standard security practice that hasn't changed.

response_text:
Good timing to ask this — OAuth2 fits into the existing design more cleanly than you might expect. The core skeleton doesn't need to be redesigned; it needs to be *extended*. Here's my thinking:

---

## The key insight: OAuth2 is identity *acquisition*, not identity *resolution*

The `AuthService` skeleton we designed handles **identity resolution** — given a credential (session, API key, token), who is the principal? OAuth2 is different: it's how you *acquire* a verified identity from a third party and translate it into a local principal.

So the question is: where does the OAuth2 flow live, and how does the result connect to what we already designed?

---

## What OAuth2 actually needs in your architecture

**1. Provider configuration** — client IDs, secrets, scopes, redirect URIs per provider. This is config, not service logic.

**2. The OAuth flow itself** — redirect to provider, handle callback, exchange code for token, fetch user info. This is thin glue code; don't write it yourself. Use [Authlib](https://docs.authlib.org/en/latest/integrations/flask.html) or [Flask-Dance](https://flask-dance.readthedocs.io/). I'd pick **Authlib** — it's more explicit and production-grade.

**3. Account linking** — given a verified external identity (`google:1234567890`), find or create a local `User`. This is the meaty part.

**4. Session creation** — once you have a local `User`, log them in exactly the same way as a password-based login.

Steps 3 and 4 belong in `AuthService`. Steps 1 and 2 belong in a new `auth/oauth.py` routes module.

---

## Concrete changes to `auth/service.py`

Add one new method group — **OAuth Account Management**:

```python
# auth/service.py

class AuthService:
    # ... existing methods unchanged ...

    # ── OAuth Account Management ──────────────────────────────────────────

    def find_or_create_oauth_user(
        self,
        provider: str,           # "google" | "github"
        provider_user_id: str,   # the stable ID from the provider (NOT email)
        email: str,
        display_name: str | None = None,
        avatar_url: str | None = None,
    ) -> tuple[User, bool]:      # (user, created)
        """
        Given a verified OAuth identity, return the local User.
        
        Lookup order:
          1. OAuthIdentity with matching (provider, provider_user_id) → return linked user
          2. User with matching email → link this provider to existing account, return user
          3. No match → create new User + OAuthIdentity, return new user

        'created' is True only if a net-new User row was made. Use it to
        trigger onboarding flow in the caller.
        
        IMPORTANT: provider_user_id is the stable numeric/opaque ID from the
        provider — NOT the email. Emails can change; provider IDs don't.
        """
        ...

    def link_oauth_identity(
        self,
        user: User,
        provider: str,
        provider_user_id: str,
    ) -> "OAuthIdentity":
        """
        Explicitly link an OAuth provider to an existing authenticated user.
        Used in account settings when a logged-in user connects a new provider.
        """
        ...

    def unlink_oauth_identity(self, user: User, provider: str) -> None:
        """
        Remove an OAuth provider link. Guards: user must have either a
        password set OR another linked provider, or they'd be locked out.
        """
        ...
```

---

## The new model: `OAuthIdentity`

You need one new table. Add it to `auth/models.py`:

```python
# auth/models.py

class OAuthIdentity(db.Model):
    __tablename__ = "oauth_identities"
    __table_args__ = (
        db.UniqueConstraint("provider", "provider_user_id", name="uq_oauth_provider_uid"),
    )

    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    user_id = db.Column(db.Integer, db.ForeignKey("users.id", ondelete="CASCADE"), nullable=False)
    provider = db.Column(db.String(32), nullable=False)          # "google", "github"
    provider_user_id = db.Column(db.String(256), nullable=False) # stable ID from provider
    email_at_link_time = db.Column(db.String(256))               # audit only, don't use for lookup
    created_at = db.Column(db.DateTime, default=datetime.utcnow)

    user = db.relationship("User", back_populates="oauth_identities")
```

Two things worth calling out:
- The unique constraint is on `(provider, provider_user_id)`, not on email. Email can change at the provider.
- `email_at_link_time` is stored for audit/support purposes only. Never use it as a lookup key.

You'll also need to update `User`:
```python
# Add to User model
oauth_identities = db.relationship("OAuthIdentity", back_populates="user", cascade="all, delete-orphan")
has_password = db.Column(db.Boolean, default=True)  # False for pure-OAuth users
```

`has_password=False` matters for `unlink_oauth_identity` — if someone signed up via Google only, they have no password, so unlinking Google locks them out.

---

## The OAuth routes: `auth/oauth.py`

This is thin glue — keep business logic out of it:

```python
# auth/oauth.py
from authlib.integrations.flask_client import OAuth
from flask import Blueprint, redirect, url_for, session, current_app
from .exceptions import AuthError

oauth_bp = Blueprint("oauth", __name__)
oauth = OAuth()

def init_oauth(app):
    oauth.init_app(app)
    oauth.register(
        name="google",
        server_metadata_url="https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration",
        client_kwargs={"scope": "openid email profile"},
    )
    oauth.register(
        name="github",
        access_token_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token",
        authorize_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize",
        api_base_url="https://api.github.com/",
        client_kwargs={"scope": "user:email"},
    )

@oauth_bp.route("/login/<provider>")
def login(provider):
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.callback", provider=provider, _external=True)
    return oauth.create_client(provider).authorize_redirect(redirect_uri)

@oauth_bp.route("/callback/<provider>")
def callback(provider):
    client = oauth.create_client(provider)
    token = client.authorize_access_token()
    
    # Normalize provider user info — each provider has different shapes
    user_info = _get_user_info(provider, client, token)
    
    auth_service = current_app.auth  # existing wiring
    user, created = auth_service.find_or_create_oauth_user(
        provider=provider,
        provider_user_id=user_info["id"],
        email=user_info["email"],
        display_name=user_info.get("name"),
    )
    
    # Reuse your existing session creation — same path as password login
    session["user_id"] = user.id
    
    if created:
        return redirect(url_for("onboarding.welcome"))
    return redirect(url_for("dashboard.index"))

def _get_user_info(provider, client, token) -> dict:
    """Normalize provider-specific user info shapes into a common dict."""
    if provider == "google":
        info = token.get("userinfo") or client.get("userinfo").json()
        return {"id": info["sub"], "email": info["email"], "name": info.get("name")}
    elif provider == "github":
        resp = client.get("user", token=token)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        # GitHub may not return email in /user — fetch separately
        email = data.get("email") or _get_github_primary_email(client, token)
        return {"id": str(data["id"]), "email": email, "name": data.get("name")}
    raise AuthError(f"Unknown OAuth provider: {provider}")

def _get_github_primary_email(client, token) -> str:
    resp = client.get("user/emails", token=token)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    emails = resp.json()
    primary = next((e["email"] for e in emails if e["primary"] and e["verified"]), None)
    if not primary:
        raise AuthError("GitHub account has no verified primary email")
    return primary
```

---

## Does the existing design need to change?

**No redesign needed.** The existing `resolve_session`, `resolve_api_key`, `resolve_service_token` are untouched. OAuth2 adds a *new concern* (account linking) but doesn't replace any existing concern. Once `find_or_create_oauth_user` runs and sets `session["user_id"]`, the existing `resolve_session` takes it from there — exactly as before.

The only thing to watch: if you currently assume `User.password_hash` is always set, you need to handle `has_password=False` users in `verify_password` and in any "change password" flows.

---

## Updated `auth/` package structure

```
auth/
├── __init__.py          # public API — unchanged
├── models.py            # + OAuthIdentity model, has_password on User
├── hashing.py           # unchanged
├── tokens.py            # unchanged
├── session.py           # unchanged
├── decorators.py        # unchanged
├── exceptions.py        # + OAuthError(AuthError) if you want provider-specific errors
├── oauth.py             # NEW — routes + provider normalization
└── service.py           # + find_or_create_oauth_user, link/unlink methods
```

---

## The edge cases that will bite you

1. **Email collision on signup**: User signs up with email `alice@example.com` via password, then later tries to log in via Google with the same email. Your `find_or_create_oauth_user` lookup-by-email path handles this — it links the provider to the existing account silently. Some teams prefer to require the user to explicitly confirm this link. Your call, but have an opinion before you ship.

2. **Email changed at provider**: Alice changes her Google email. Next OAuth login, `provider_user_id` still matches, so she gets her account. `email_at_link_time` diverges from her current email. Don't auto-update `User.email` from OAuth without confirmation — it's a potential account takeover vector.

3. **GitHub email privacy**: GitHub users can hide their email. The `_get_github_primary_email` fallback handles this, but you should also decide: is a verified email required? I'd say yes, and raise `AuthError` if none is available.

4. **CSRF on the callback**: Authlib handles the `state` parameter automatically, but make sure you're not stripping it in any middleware.

---

## Where this fits in the implementation plan

This becomes step 0c — parallel to the performance work, before the full refactor:

- `0b` (in progress): join query + cache for API key performance
- `0c` (new): `OAuthIdentity` migration + `find_or_create_oauth_user` + `auth/oauth.py` routes
- Steps 1–8: unchanged

The OAuth work is largely independent of the performance work and can go in a separate PR.

What's your current session management — Flask-Login or custom? That affects how `session["user_id"]` gets set in the callback.

Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and presumably no shared test coverage. The 8K-line codebase is poorly tested, making this a high-risk refactor without a clear seam. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation in api/routes.py checks only the key itself, not the associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still work. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. The fix must not conflate human users and service accounts. The security fix has been applied and works. New performance concern: the fix adds 2 extra DB queries per API request, unacceptable on high-traffic endpoints. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement.

Silent email-based account linking on first OAuth login is the default — explicit confirmation is an optional stricter policy

strands.title: API Key Performance — Query Reduction and Caching
strands.content: The security fix introduced 2 queries per API request (key lookup + user status check). Three layers of optimization are available, in order of preference: (1) JOIN FIRST: Collapse the two queries into one joined query in resolve_api_key. Expected: ~0.3–0.5ms. Do this regardless. Measure before adding a cache. (2) SHORT TTL CACHE (Option A): Cache the (user_id, api_key_id) tuple keyed by key_hash in Redis/Flask-Caching with a 30s TTL. On cache hit, reload ORM objects by PK and re-check is_active + revoked in Python as safety net. (3) EXPLICIT INVALIDATION (Option B): When admin disables a user, bust cache entries explicitly. Required if compliance mandates immediate revocation. (4) ASYNC last_used_at WRITE: Synchronous last_used_at write is likely doing more damage than the user query. Fire-and-forget or Redis list + bulk flush every 60s. CRITICAL: TTL value and security implications must be documented at the call site.

30-second TTL cache is the right default — short enough to be defensible, long enough to matter
Cache stores (user_id, api_key_id) not ORM objects — ORM objects are not safely serializable across requests
Synchronous last_used_at write is likely the larger performance problem — async/bulk flush is the fix
TTL semantics must be documented at the call site — not README — with explicit revocation escape hatch

strands.content: Both security bugs should be fixed now, surgically, before the refactor. The API key bug fix has a critical constraint: service accounts share the key resolution path and must not be broken by an is_active check. Fix strategy: branch on key type at point of user resolution — only human user keys get the is_active check; service account keys skip it by design. Three likely schema patterns determine the exact implementation: (1) separate tables; (2) nullable user_id and service_account_id; (3) key_type discriminator. In all cases, the fix returns a (principal, api_key) tuple. The fix is confirmed working. Performance is the current parallel concern.

user_message: OK so we need auth/service.py to handle three flows: password, API key, and OAuth2. Write me the updated skeleton with all three. The password flow should use the existing bcrypt hashing from models.py. The OAuth2 flow needs a new `auth/oauth.py` module. Keep the API key flow simple.

strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and presumably no shared test coverage. The 8K-line codebase is poorly tested, making this a high-risk refactor without a clear seam. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation in api/routes.py checks only the key itself, not the associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still work. CRITICAL CONSTRAINT: service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. The fix must not conflate human users and service accounts. The security fix has been applied and works. New performance concern: the fix adds 2 extra DB queries per API request, unacceptable on high-traffic endpoints. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed but adds 2 DB queries per request
g.user caching is not the bug — Flask's g is request-scoped and always invalidated at request end
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a new product requirement added this cycle

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content: Recommended: a single auth/ package. Structure: __init__.py (public API), models.py (User + credential models + OAuthIdentity), hashing.py (password hashing utils), tokens.py (API key + service account token validation), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three auth decorators), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy + OAuthError subclass), service.py (central AuthService class), oauth.py (NEW — OAuth routes + provider normalization). The auth/service.py module is the gravitational center for identity resolution. The caching layer belongs in AuthService.resolve_api_key. OAuth2 adds a new concern (account linking / identity acquisition) but does not replace or redesign any existing concern — it extends service.py with a new method group and adds oauth.py for routes.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
AuthService must have distinct resolve paths for user API keys and service account tokens
Wire AuthService once in app factory, access via current_app.auth
Cache lives in AuthService.resolve_api_key — not in the decorator — for testability and encapsulation
OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not identity resolution — it feeds into existing session path once complete
auth/oauth.py is thin glue — business logic belongs in AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user

strands.title: AuthService Interface
strands.content: auth/service.py skeleton. Public method groups: (1) Identity Resolution — resolve_session(user_id) -> User, resolve_api_key(raw_key) -> tuple[User, ApiKey], resolve_service_token(raw_token) -> ServiceAccount. (2) Credential Management — create_api_key, revoke_api_key, rotate_service_token. (3) Password Operations — verify_password, set_password. (4) Cache Management — invalidate_user_key_cache(user_id). (5) OAuth Account Management (NEW) — find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) -> tuple[User, bool]; link_oauth_identity(user, provider, provider_user_id) -> OAuthIdentity; unlink_oauth_identity(user, provider) -> None. resolve_api_key always checks is_active; resolve_service_token never does. find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: (1) OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id); (2) User by email → link silently; (3) create new User + OAuthIdentity. unlink_oauth_identity must guard against lockout: user must have has_password=True OR another linked provider.

key_claims.text: resolve_api_key returns (User, ApiKey) tuple — decorator needs both to update last_used_at
resolve_service_token is a distinct method — service accounts bypass is_active check by design
invalidate_user_key_cache is a first-class method — called from admin actions, not just internally
find_or_create_oauth_user uses provider_user_id (stable) not email as primary lookup key
unlink_oauth_identity must check has_password or other linked providers before removing
create_api_key raw key returned once and never stored

strands.title: OAuth2 Integration
strands.content: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) fits into the existing auth/service.py design as a new method group — no redesign needed. The pattern: OAuth flow (in auth/oauth.py) acquires a verified external identity → AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user() links it to a local User → existing resolve_session() takes it from there. Library choice: Authlib (preferred over Flask-Dance — more explicit, production-grade). Provider normalization lives in auth/oauth.py._get_user_info() — each provider has different response shapes. New model: OAuthIdentity (auth/models.py) with unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id). New User field: has_password (boolean, False for pure-OAuth signups). Email collision handling: if a User with the same email exists, link the provider silently — teams may prefer explicit confirmation; this is a deliberate policy choice. Key edge cases: (1) email changed at provider — don't auto-update User.email, potential account takeover vector; (2) GitHub email privacy — must call /user/emails endpoint for verified primary email; (3) CSRF — Authlib handles state param automatically; (4) lockout guard on unlink. Email is stored as email_at_link_time for audit only — never used as a lookup key after initial account matching.

raw_output.response:
Here's the full skeleton. I'll give you `auth/service.py` first, then `auth/oauth.py`. I've kept the API key flow deliberately simple — a single joined query, no cache added yet until you profile. The OAuth flow delegates all the heavy lifting to `AuthService`, keeping `oauth.py` as thin glue.

---

## `auth/service.py`

```python
# auth/service.py
from __future__ import annotations

import hashlib
import hmac
import secrets
from typing import TYPE_CHECKING

from flask import current_app
from sqlalchemy.orm import joinedload

from auth.exceptions import (
    AccountDisabledError,
    InvalidCredentialsError,
    InvalidTokenError,
    OAuthAccountLinkError,
    OAuthLockoutError,
)
from auth.models import ApiKey, OAuthIdentity, ServiceAccount, User

if TYPE_CHECKING:
    from flask_sqlalchemy import SQLAlchemy


class AuthService:
    """
    Central authority for all identity resolution.

    Three resolution paths:
      - Password     → verify_password(email, raw_password) → User
      - API key      → resolve_api_key(raw_key) → (User | ServiceAccount, ApiKey)
      - OAuth        → find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, ...) → (User, created: bool)

    Wire once in your app factory:
        app.auth = AuthService(db)

    Access via:
        from flask import current_app
        svc = current_app.auth
    """

    def __init__(self, db: "SQLAlchemy") -> None:
        self._db = db

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 1. PASSWORD FLOW
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def verify_password(self, email: str, raw_password: str) -> User:
        """
        Validates email + password credentials.

        Uses bcrypt from the existing User model — we don't reimplement
        hashing here, we delegate to User.check_password() which wraps
        bcrypt.checkpw(). This keeps the hashing logic in one place.

        Raises:
            InvalidCredentialsError: if email not found or password wrong.
                Intentionally returns the same error for both to prevent
                user enumeration.
            AccountDisabledError: if credentials are valid but account is
                disabled. Deliberately raised AFTER credential check so
                that a bad actor can't use this to enumerate valid emails.
        """
        user: User | None = (
            self._db.session.query(User)
            .filter(User.email == email.lower().strip())
            .first()
        )

        # Use constant-time comparison path even when user is None
        # to prevent timing-based email enumeration.
        dummy_hash = "$2b$12$invalidhashfortimingpurposesonly000000000000000000000000"
        candidate_hash = user.password_hash if user else dummy_hash

        # Delegates to User.check_password → bcrypt.checkpw
        password_valid = User.check_password_hash(candidate_hash, raw_password)

        if not user or not password_valid:
            raise InvalidCredentialsError("Invalid email or password.")

        if not user.is_active:
            raise AccountDisabledError(f"Account {user.id} is disabled.")

        return user

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 2. API KEY FLOW
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def resolve_api_key(self, raw_key: str) -> tuple[User | ServiceAccount, ApiKey]:
        """
        Resolves a raw API key to its principal (User or ServiceAccount)
        and the ApiKey record.

        Single joined query — no N+1. The join fetches the associated
        User or ServiceAccount in the same round trip.

        Key design decisions:
          - Human user keys:      is_active check enforced.
          - Service account keys: is_active check SKIPPED by design.
            Service accounts have no disabled flag. If you add one later,
            add resolve_service_token() as a distinct method.
          - Returns (principal, api_key) tuple so the caller (decorator)
            can update api_key.last_used_at without a second query.

        Raises:
            InvalidTokenError: if key doesn't exist, is revoked, or the
                associated human user is disabled.

        NOTE: last_used_at is updated synchronously here. If this becomes
        a performance bottleneck, move to async (see performance notes in
        API Key Performance strand). Do not add caching until you've
        profiled — the join query alone may be sufficient.
        """
        key_hash = self._hash_key(raw_key)

        api_key: ApiKey | None = (
            self._db.session.query(ApiKey)
            .filter(ApiKey.key_hash == key_hash, ApiKey.revoked == False)  # noqa: E712
            .options(
                joinedload(ApiKey.user),
                joinedload(ApiKey.service_account),
            )
            .first()
        )

        if api_key is None:
            raise InvalidTokenError("API key not found or revoked.")

        # Branch on principal type — human users get is_active check;
        # service accounts do not.
        if api_key.service_account_id is not None:
            principal = api_key.service_account
        else:
            user = api_key.user
            if user is None:
                # Orphaned key — data integrity problem, treat as invalid.
                raise InvalidTokenError("API key has no associated principal.")
            if not user.is_active:
                raise AccountDisabledError(
                    f"User {user.id} is disabled; API key rejected."
                )
            principal = user

        # Synchronous write — see docstring note about async if needed.
        self._touch_api_key(api_key)

        return principal, api_key

    def create_api_key(self, user: User, name: str) -> tuple[ApiKey, str]:
        """
        Generates a new API key for a human user.

        Returns (ApiKey, raw_key). The raw key is returned ONCE and never
        stored — only the hash is persisted. Show it to the user now or
        it's gone.
        """
        raw_key = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
        key_hash = self._hash_key(raw_key)

        api_key = ApiKey(
            user_id=user.id,
            name=name,
            key_hash=key_hash,
            revoked=False,
        )
        self._db.session.add(api_key)
        self._db.session.commit()

        return api_key, raw_key

    def revoke_api_key(self, api_key: ApiKey) -> None:
        api_key.revoked = True
        self._db.session.commit()

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # 3. OAUTH FLOW
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    def find_or_create_oauth_user(
        self,
        *,
        provider: str,
        provider_user_id: str,
        email: str,
        display_name: str,
        avatar_url: str | None = None,
    ) -> tuple[User, bool]:
        """
        Resolves an OAuth callback to a local User. Returns (user, created).

        Lookup order — deliberate, don't reorder:
          1. OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id) — the stable key.
             Provider user IDs never change; emails do.
          2. User by email — existing password account, link silently.
             POLICY: silent linking is the default. If your team prefers
             explicit confirmation (show a "link accounts?" screen), return
             a sentinel here and handle it in oauth.py instead.
          3. Create new User + OAuthIdentity.

        SECURITY: Never auto-update User.email from provider data after
        initial creation. If a provider's email changes, that's the user's
        problem to resolve — auto-updating is an account takeover vector
        (attacker claims your email at the provider, hijacks your account).

        Email stored in OAuthIdentity.email_at_link_time for audit only.

        Raises:
            OAuthAccountLinkError: if linking would violate a constraint
                (e.g., this provider_user_id is already linked to a
                different account).
        """
        # Step 1: Look up by stable provider identity.
        identity: OAuthIdentity | None = (
            self._db.session.query(OAuthIdentity)
            .filter_by(provider=provider, provider_user_id=str(provider_user_id))
            .options(joinedload(OAuthIdentity.user))
            .first()
        )

        if identity is not None:
            # Fast path — we've seen this provider identity before.
            return identity.user, False

        # Step 2: Email match — existing user, link the new provider.
        normalized_email = email.lower().strip()
        existing_user: User | None = (
            self._db.session.query(User)
            .filter(User.email == normalized_email)
            .first()
        )

        if existing_user is not None:
            new_identity = self._create_oauth_identity(
                user=existing_user,
                provider=provider,
                provider_user_id=str(provider_user_id),
                email_at_link_time=normalized_email,
            )
            self._db.session.add(new_identity)
            self._db.session.commit()
            return existing_user, False

        # Step 3: Brand new user.
        new_user = User(
            email=normalized_email,
            display_name=display_name,
            avatar_url=avatar_url,
            has_password=False,  # Pure-OAuth signup — no password set.
            is_active=True,
        )
        self._db.session.add(new_user)
        self._db.session.flush()  # Get new_user.id before creating identity.

        new_identity = self._create_oauth_identity(
            user=new_user,
            provider=provider,
            provider_user_id=str(provider_user_id),
            email_at_link_time=normalized_email,
        )
        self._db.session.add(new_identity)
        self._db.session.commit()

        return new_user, True

    def link_oauth_identity(
        self,
        user: User,
        provider: str,
        provider_user_id: str,
    ) -> OAuthIdentity:
        """
        Links a new OAuth provider to an existing authenticated user
        (e.g., from an account settings page).

        Raises:
            OAuthAccountLinkError: if this (provider, provider_user_id)
                is already linked to a different user.
        """
        existing: OAuthIdentity | None = (
            self._db.session.query(OAuthIdentity)
            .filter_by(provider=provider, provider_user_id=str(provider_user_id))
            .first()
        )

        if existing is not None and existing.user_id != user.id:
            raise OAuthAccountLinkError(
                f"This {provider} account is already linked to a different user."
            )

        if existing is not None:
            # Already linked to this user — idempotent.
            return existing

        identity = self._create_oauth_identity(
            user=user,
            provider=provider,
            provider_user_id=str(provider_user_id),
            email_at_link_time=None,
        )
        self._db.session.add(identity)
        self._db.session.commit()
        return identity

    def unlink_oauth_identity(self, user: User, provider: str) -> None:
        """
        Removes an OAuth provider link from a user's account.

        Lockout guard: refuses if this would leave the user with no way
        to log in. Specifically: user must have has_password=True OR
        at least one other linked provider after this removal.

        Raises:
            OAuthLockoutError: if unlinking would lock the user out.
        """
        identity: OAuthIdentity | None = (
            self._db.session.query(OAuthIdentity)
            .filter_by(user_id=user.id, provider=provider)
            .first()
        )

        if identity is None:
            return  # Idempotent — nothing to do.

        # Lockout guard.
        other_providers = (
            self._db.session.query(OAuthIdentity)
            .filter(OAuthIdentity.user_id == user.id, OAuthIdentity.provider != provider)
            .count()
        )

        if not user.has_password and other_providers == 0:
            raise OAuthLockoutError(
                f"Cannot unlink {provider}: user has no password and no other "
                "login methods. Set a password first."
            )

        self._db.session.delete(identity)
        self._db.session.commit()

    # ------------------------------------------------------------------
    # INTERNALS
    # ------------------------------------------------------------------

    @staticmethod
    def _hash_key(raw_key: str) -> str:
        """
        SHA-256 hash of the raw key for storage and lookup.
        Not bcrypt — bcrypt is too slow for per-request key lookup.
        Raw keys are high-entropy (secrets.token_urlsafe(32)), so
        a fast hash is safe here.
        """
        return hashlib.sha256(raw_key.encode()).hexdigest()

    @staticmethod
    def _create_oauth_identity(
        *,
        user: User,
        provider: str,
        provider_user_id: str,
        email_at_link_time: str | None,
    ) -> OAuthIdentity:
        return OAuthIdentity(
            user_id=user.id,
            provider=provider,
            provider_user_id=provider_user_id,
            email_at_link_time=email_at_link_time,
        )

    def _touch_api_key(self, api_key: ApiKey) -> None:
        """Synchronous last_used_at update. Make async if this shows in profiling."""
        from datetime import datetime, timezone

        api_key.last_used_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
        self._db.session.commit()
```

## `auth/oauth.py`

```python
# auth/oauth.py
#
# Thin glue layer. No business logic lives here.
# All account linking/creation is in AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user().
#
# Requires: pip install authlib
# Register blueprint in your app factory:
#     from auth.oauth import oauth_bp
#     app.register_blueprint(oauth_bp)

from __future__ import annotations

from authlib.integrations.flask_client import OAuth
from flask import Blueprint, abort, current_app, redirect, session, url_for

from auth.exceptions import OAuthAccountLinkError

oauth_bp = Blueprint("oauth", __name__, url_prefix="/auth")

# Module-level OAuth client — registered in init_oauth().
_oauth = OAuth()


def init_oauth(app) -> None:
    """
    Call this in your app factory after app.auth = AuthService(db).

        from auth.oauth import init_oauth
        init_oauth(app)

    Required config keys (set in your Flask config / env):
        GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET
        GITHUB_CLIENT_ID, GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET
        SECRET_KEY  (used by Authlib for state/CSRF — already required by Flask)
    """
    _oauth.init_app(app)

    _oauth.register(
        name="google",
        client_id=app.config["GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID"],
        client_secret=app.config["GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET"],
        server_metadata_url="https://accounts.google.com/.well-known/openid-configuration",
        client_kwargs={"scope": "openid email profile"},
    )

    _oauth.register(
        name="github",
        client_id=app.config["GITHUB_CLIENT_ID"],
        client_secret=app.config["GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET"],
        access_token_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token",
        authorize_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize",
        api_base_url="https://api.github.com/",
        client_kwargs={"scope": "read:user user:email"},
    )


# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Routes — one pair per provider: /authorize + /callback
# ------------------------------------------------------------------

@oauth_bp.route("/login/google")
def google_login():
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.google_callback", _external=True)
    return _oauth.google.authorize_redirect(redirect_uri)


@oauth_bp.route("/callback/google")
def google_callback():
    token = _oauth.google.authorize_access_token()
    user_info = token.get("userinfo")  # Authlib parses the OIDC id_token for us.

    if not user_info or not user_info.get("email_verified"):
        abort(400, "Google did not return a verified email address.")

    provider_data = {
        "provider": "google",
        "provider_user_id": user_info["sub"],  # Stable Google account ID.
        "email": user_info["email"],
        "display_name": user_info.get("name", ""),
        "avatar_url": user_info.get("picture"),
    }

    return _complete_oauth_login(provider_data)


@oauth_bp.route("/login/github")
def github_login():
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.github_callback", _external=True)
    return _oauth.github.authorize_redirect(redirect_uri)


@oauth_bp.route("/callback/github")
def github_callback():
    _oauth.github.authorize_access_token()

    # /user alone may not include email if the user has it private.
    # Always call /user/emails and find the verified primary.
    user_data = _oauth.github.get("user").json()
    email = _get_github_primary_email()

    if not email:
        abort(400, "Could not retrieve a verified primary email from GitHub.")

    provider_data = {
        "provider": "github",
        "provider_user_id": str(user_data["id"]),  # Stable GitHub numeric user ID.
        "email": email,
        "display_name": user_data.get("name") or user_data.get("login", ""),
        "avatar_url": user_data.get("avatar_url"),
    }

    return _complete_oauth_login(provider_data)


# ------------------------------------------------------------------
# Shared callback logic
# ------------------------------------------------------------------

def _complete_oauth_login(provider_data: dict) -> "Response":
    """
    Final step for all OAuth providers.

    Calls AuthService, sets the session, redirects.
    This is where you'd branch if you want explicit account-linking
    confirmation instead of silent linking — check `created` or add
    a "pending_link" session key and redirect to a confirmation view.
    """
    try:
        user, created = current_app.auth.find_or_create_oauth_user(**provider_data)
    except OAuthAccountLinkError as e:
        # This provider identity is already linked to a different account.
        # In practice this shouldn't happen in the normal login flow,
        # but handle it gracefully.
        abort(409, str(e))

    # Session management — see note below about Flask-Login.
    _set_user_session(user)

    # Redirect to post-login destination or default.
    next_url = session.pop("next", None) or url_for("main.dashboard")
    return redirect(next_url)


def _set_user_session(user) -> None:
    """
    IMPORTANT: This implementation assumes raw session management.
    If you're using Flask-Login, replace this with:
        from flask_login import login_user
        login_user(user, remember=True)

    We don't know yet which you're using — this is the #1 open question
    before you ship oauth.py. Check your app factory for `LoginManager`.
    """
    session.clear()  # Prevent session fixation.
    session["user_id"] = user.id


def _get_github_primary_email() -> str | None:
    """
    GitHub users can set their email as private, in which case /user
    returns null for the email field. /user/emails returns all addresses
    with verification status. We want the verified primary.
    """
    emails = _oauth.github.get("user/emails").json()
    for entry in emails:
        if entry.get("primary") and entry.get("verified"):
            return entry["email"]
    return None
```

---

## A few things to call out explicitly:

**The `_set_user_session` function is a land mine.** I've put a big comment on it, but I'll say it here too: if `LoginManager` is anywhere in your app factory, replace those two lines with `login_user(user, remember=True)`. Flask-Login handles session fixation protection, "remember me" cookies, and `@login_required` integration — raw `session["user_id"]` will silently bypass all of that. **This is the one thing I'd block a PR on before shipping.**

**`_hash_key` uses SHA-256, not bcrypt.** This is intentional. bcrypt is deliberately slow (that's the point for passwords), but API keys are 256 bits of entropy from `secrets.token_urlsafe` — a fast hash is cryptographically safe here. Don't let anyone "fix" this to bcrypt; it would make your API unusable under any real load.

**The join query in `resolve_api_key` uses `joinedload`** so you get the user (or service account) in a single round trip. The `service_account_id is not None` branch is the seam for the is_active bypass — one obvious place to read and audit.

**`find_or_create_oauth_user` uses `flush()` before creating the `OAuthIdentity`** so that `new_user.id` is populated by the DB before it's referenced as a foreign key. Don't collapse that into a single `commit()` — you'll get an integrity error.

What's still open: once you tell me whether you're using Flask-Login, I can give you the exact `_set_user_session` implementation and the `@login_required` decorator update for the OAuth path.

strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and presumably no shared test coverage. The 8K-line codebase is poorly tested, making this a high-risk refactor without a clear seam. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified and fixed: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation did not check associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still worked. Service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. The fix does not conflate them. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement. New performance concern: the fix adds 2 extra DB queries per API request, now collapsed to a single joined query.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed with a single joined query
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a confirmed product requirement

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content: Recommended: a single auth/ package. Structure: __init__.py (public API), models.py (User + credential models + OAuthIdentity), hashing.py (password hashing utils), tokens.py (API key + service account token validation), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three auth decorators), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy + OAuthError subclass), service.py (central AuthService class), oauth.py (OAuth routes + provider normalization). auth/service.py is the gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService, wired once in app factory via current_app.auth. OAuth2 adds identity acquisition (not resolution) — it feeds into existing resolve_session path once complete. auth/oauth.py is thin glue; all business logic is in AuthService.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
Wire AuthService once in app factory, access via current_app.auth
OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not identity resolution — it feeds into existing session path once complete
auth/oauth.py is thin glue — business logic belongs in AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user

strands.title: AuthService — Implemented Skeleton
strands.content: auth/service.py skeleton has been written in full. Three method groups: (1) Password: verify_password(email, raw_password) → User. Delegates to User.check_password_hash (bcrypt from existing models.py). Constant-time path even when user is None to prevent timing-based email enumeration. Checks is_active AFTER credential validation to avoid valid-email disclosure. (2) API key: resolve_api_key(raw_key) → (User|ServiceAccount, ApiKey). Single joined query with joinedload(ApiKey.user) + joinedload(ApiKey.service_account). Branches on api_key.service_account_id — human users get is_active check; service accounts bypass it. Synchronous last_used_at write via _touch_api_key (async later if profiling shows need). create_api_key returns (ApiKey, raw_key) — raw key returned once, never stored. Key hashing via SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are high-entropy, bcrypt would be a per-request performance disaster. (3) OAuth: find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) → (User, bool). Lookup order: OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id) → User by email (silent link) → create new User. flush() before OAuthIdentity creation to populate new_user.id as FK. Never updates User.email after creation (account takeover vector). link_oauth_identity and unlink_oauth_identity with lockout guard (has_password OR another provider). Cache layer deferred — join query alone may be sufficient.

key_claims.text: verify_password uses constant-time path even when user is None — prevents timing-based email enumeration
resolve_api_key uses a single joined query — no N+1
API key hashing uses SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are 256-bit entropy, fast hash is safe
find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: identity by provider_user_id → user by email → create new
flush() before OAuthIdentity creation is required to get new_user.id as FK — do not collapse to commit()
unlink_oauth_identity lockout guard: requires has_password=True OR another linked provider
Never auto-update User.email from provider data — account takeover vector

strands.title: auth/oauth.py — Implemented Skeleton
strands.content: auth/oauth.py is a thin Blueprint using Authlib (not Flask-Dance). init_oauth(app) registers Google (OIDC discovery URL, scope: openid email profile) and GitHub (explicit URLs, scope: read:user user:email). Route pairs: /auth/login/{provider} + /auth/callback/{provider}. Google: parses userinfo from OIDC id_token via Authlib (no extra API call needed); checks email_verified. GitHub: calls /user for profile + /user/emails for verified primary email (_get_github_primary_email) because GitHub email may be private. Both converge in _complete_oauth_login which calls AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user then _set_user_session. CSRF handled automatically by Authlib via state param. CRITICAL UNRESOLVED: _set_user_session is currently raw session management (session['user_id'] = user.id). If Flask-Login is in use, this must be replaced with login_user(user, remember=True) — Flask-Login handles session fixation, remember-me cookies, and @login_required. This is a PR-blocking question. next_url preserved across OAuth flow via session['next'].

GitHub requires /user/emails call for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable
Google OIDC userinfo is parsed from id_token by Authlib — no separate /userinfo call needed
_set_user_session is a PR-blocking unknown — Flask-Login vs raw session must be resolved
session.clear() before setting user_id prevents session fixation

strands.title: API Key Performance — Query Reduction and Caching
strands.content: The security fix introduced 2 queries per API request. Join query (joinedload in resolve_api_key) collapses this to one — no semantic tradeoff, unconditionally applied in the skeleton. Cache deferred until profiling confirms need. If cache is added: store (user_id, api_key_id) keyed by key_hash, 30s TTL, re-check is_active + revoked in Python on cache hit. Async last_used_at write (_touch_api_key) is the next lever if join alone is insufficient — fire-and-forget or Redis list + bulk flush. TTL semantics must be documented at call site with explicit revocation escape hatch.

Cache deferred until profiling — join may be sufficient
Synchronous last_used_at write (_touch_api_key) is the next performance lever after profiling

strands.title: OAuth2 Integration — Design Decisions
strands.content: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) fits into auth/service.py as a new method group — no redesign needed. OAuthIdentity model (auth/models.py): unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id), stores email_at_link_time for audit only (never used as lookup key post-creation). User.has_password: boolean, False for pure-OAuth signups, required for lockout guard. Email collision handling: silent linking by default; explicit confirmation is optional stricter policy. Email-based auto-update post-creation is prohibited (account takeover vector). GitHub email privacy handled via /user/emails endpoint. CSRF via Authlib state param. Lockout guard on unlink enforced in service layer.

has_password=False required on User for pure-OAuth signups and lockout guard
Silent email-based account linking on first OAuth login is the default — explicit confirmation is optional
Auto-updating User.email from OAuth provider is a potential account takeover vector — prohibited
