strands.title: OAuth2 Integration — Design Decisions
strands.content: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) fits into auth/service.py as a new method group — no redesign needed. OAuthIdentity model (auth/models.py): unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id), stores email_at_link_time for audit only (never used as lookup key post-creation). User.has_password: boolean, False for pure-OAuth signups, required for lockout guard. Email collision handling: silent linking by default; explicit confirmation is optional stricter policy. Email-based auto-update post-creation is prohibited (account takeover vector). GitHub email privacy handled via /user/emails endpoint. CSRF via Authlib state param. Lockout guard on unlink enforced in service layer.

key_claims.text: OAuthIdentity unique constraint on (provider, provider_user_id) — not email
has_password=False required on User for pure-OAuth signups and lockout guard
Silent email-based account linking on first OAuth login is the default — explicit confirmation is optional
Auto-updating User.email from OAuth provider is a potential account takeover vector — prohibited

strands.title: Unknown Unknowns
strands.content: Remaining unknowns: (1) Flask-Login vs custom session management — PR-blocking for oauth.py; determines _set_user_session implementation. (2) Service account schema — same table vs separate, discriminator vs nullable FK; determines resolve_api_key branch logic. (3) Redis in stack — determines cache backend if profiling shows cache is needed. (4) Compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation — determines whether explicit cache invalidation is required. (5) Email collision policy preference — silent link (current default) vs explicit confirmation. (6) has_password column migration — existing users need default=True backfill. (7) Test infrastructure: pytest + SQLite or heavier? App factory exists or module-level construction?

Existing users need has_password=True backfill in the migration — do not default to False
Whether a create_app factory exists determines Layer 2 test fixture complexity

open_questions: Are you using Flask-Login (LoginManager) or raw session management? This is a merge blocker for auth/oauth.py — determines whether _set_user_session uses login_user(user) or session['user_id'] = user.id.
What is the ApiKey/ServiceAccount schema? (discriminator column, nullable FKs, or separate tables) — determines the branching logic in resolve_api_key.
Is Redis already in the stack? Determines cache backend if profiling shows the join query alone is insufficient.
Do you have compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation on user disable? Determines whether a cache TTL is acceptable or explicit invalidation is required.
Email collision policy: silent linking (current default in skeleton) or explicit user confirmation screen when an OAuth email matches an existing password account?
Does your User model already have a has_password field, or does the migration need to add it with a default=True backfill for existing users?
Test infrastructure: are you using pytest + SQLite, or something heavier (Postgres test DB, Docker)? Does a create_app factory already exist or is the app constructed at module level?
tensor.instructions_for_next: Testing strategy is now documented. Most likely next questions: (a) Flask-Login answer — still the top blocker; (b) whether create_app factory exists (affects Layer 2 test fixture complexity significantly — module-level app means tests need app_context() gymnastics); (c) they may ask about mocking Authlib for OAuth tests more concretely (the fake JWT for Google id_token is non-trivial); (d) they may ask about pytest-flask vs raw test client; (e) they may ask about CI integration and whether to gate on auth_security marker. Watch for: if they don't have a create_app factory, Layer 2 tests become harder — they need to either add one (recommended) or use a different fixture pattern. The `responses` library mock for OAuth tests requires `pip install responses` — they may not have it. Also: if test DB is Postgres-only (no SQLite), the Layer 2 fixtures need adjustment (Postgres docker-compose for CI, or pytest-postgresql). The timing-attack test is subtle — if they ask, note that it can only truly test the code path, not the actual timing, since Python mock timing is non-deterministic.

user_message: Bad news: the ops team says we can't add any new dependencies (like an OAuth library) until the next release cycle. But product wants OAuth2 in THIS release. The ops team is firm — they just had an incident caused by a transitive dependency. Can we implement OAuth2 without adding a library? How much code is that?

strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and no test coverage. The 8K-line codebase has zero auth tests; only integration tests that log in with a hardcoded test user exist. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified and fixed: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation did not check associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still worked. Service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement. The security fix introduced 2 extra DB queries per API request, now collapsed to a single joined query.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Zero auth tests exist — only integration tests with a hardcoded test user
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed with a single joined query
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a confirmed product requirement

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content: Recommended: a single auth/ package. Structure: __init__.py (public API), models.py (User + credential models + OAuthIdentity), hashing.py (password hashing utils), tokens.py (API key + service account token validation), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three auth decorators), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy + OAuthError subclass), service.py (central AuthService class), oauth.py (OAuth routes + provider normalization). auth/service.py is the gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService, wired once in app factory via current_app.auth. OAuth2 adds identity acquisition (not resolution) — it feeds into existing resolve_session path once complete. auth/oauth.py is thin glue; all business logic is in AuthService.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
Wire AuthService once in app factory, access via current_app.auth
OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not identity resolution — it feeds into existing session path once complete
auth/oauth.py is thin glue — business logic belongs in AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user

strands.title: AuthService — Implemented Skeleton
strands.content: auth/service.py skeleton has been written in full. Three method groups: (1) Password: verify_password(email, raw_password) → User. Delegates to User.check_password_hash (bcrypt from existing models.py). Constant-time path even when user is None to prevent timing-based email enumeration. Checks is_active AFTER credential validation to avoid valid-email disclosure. (2) API key: resolve_api_key(raw_key) → (User|ServiceAccount, ApiKey). Single joined query with joinedload(ApiKey.user) + joinedload(ApiKey.service_account). Branches on api_key.service_account_id — human users get is_active check; service accounts bypass it. Synchronous last_used_at write via _touch_api_key (async later if profiling shows need). create_api_key returns (ApiKey, raw_key) — raw key returned once, never stored. Key hashing via SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are high-entropy, bcrypt would be a per-request performance disaster. (3) OAuth: find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) → (User, bool). Lookup order: OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id) → User by email (silent link) → create new User. flush() before OAuthIdentity creation to populate new_user.id as FK. Never updates User.email after creation (account takeover vector). link_oauth_identity and unlink_oauth_identity with lockout guard (has_password OR another provider). Cache layer deferred — join query alone may be sufficient.

key_claims.text: verify_password uses constant-time path even when user is None — prevents timing-based email enumeration
resolve_api_key uses a single joined query — no N+1
API key hashing uses SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are 256-bit entropy, fast hash is safe
find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: identity by provider_user_id → user by email → create new
flush() before OAuthIdentity creation is required to get new_user.id as FK — do not collapse to commit()
unlink_oauth_identity lockout guard: requires has_password=True OR another linked provider
Never auto-update User.email from provider data — account takeover vector

raw_output.response:
Ouch. That's a real constraint, and the ops team is right to be cautious after a dep incident. The short answer is: **yes, you can implement OAuth2 without Authlib or Flask-Dance, but you're going to write a non-trivial amount of security-sensitive code by hand.** Let me be direct about the tradeoffs before you decide.

---

## What OAuth2 actually is, stripped down

For your use case (Google + GitHub login), the flow is:

1. **Authorization redirect** — build a URL with `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `scope`, `state`, `response_type=code`. Redirect user there.
2. **Callback** — receive `code` + `state` from provider. Verify `state` matches what you sent.
3. **Token exchange** — POST to provider's token endpoint with `code`, get back `access_token` (and for Google: `id_token`).
4. **User info fetch** — call provider's API with `access_token` to get user profile.
5. **Your code** — call `find_or_create_oauth_user(...)`, set session.

The standard library covers all the HTTP mechanics. What a library like Authlib gives you beyond that:

- **PKCE** (code verifier/challenge generation)
- **JWT validation** of Google's `id_token` (signature verification against JWKS)
- **State param generation and CSRF protection** (trivial to replicate)
- **Provider config discovery** (OIDC discovery URL parsing)
- **Token refresh** (you don't need this for login-only)

The hard part you cannot safely skip: **Google's `id_token` JWT validation**.

---

## The real cost breakdown

### What's easy (stdlib only, ~50 lines total)

```python
# auth/oauth_client.py

import hashlib
import hmac
import json
import os
import secrets
import urllib.parse
import urllib.request

class OAuthClient:
    """Minimal OAuth2 client. No external dependencies."""

    def __init__(self, client_id, client_secret, authorize_url,
                 token_url, userinfo_url=None, scope=""):
        self.client_id = client_id
        self.client_secret = client_secret
        self.authorize_url = authorize_url
        self.token_url = token_url
        self.userinfo_url = userinfo_url
        self.scope = scope

    def get_authorize_url(self, redirect_uri, state):
        params = {
            "client_id": self.client_id,
            "redirect_uri": redirect_uri,
            "response_type": "code",
            "scope": self.scope,
            "state": state,
        }
        return self.authorize_url + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)

    def exchange_code(self, code, redirect_uri):
        data = urllib.parse.urlencode({
            "client_id": self.client_id,
            "client_secret": self.client_secret,
            "code": code,
            "redirect_uri": redirect_uri,
            "grant_type": "authorization_code",
        }).encode()
        req = urllib.request.Request(
            self.token_url,
            data=data,
            headers={"Accept": "application/json"},
            method="POST",
        )
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:
            return json.loads(resp.read())

    def get_userinfo(self, access_token):
        req = urllib.request.Request(
            self.userinfo_url,
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
        )
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:
            return json.loads(resp.read())
```

State management (CSRF protection):

```python
def generate_state():
    return secrets.token_urlsafe(32)

def verify_state(session_state, callback_state):
    if not session_state or not callback_state:
        return False
    return hmac.compare_digest(session_state, callback_state)
```

**This part is fine.** It's just HTTP. ~80 lines total. Readable, testable, no magic.

---

### What's hard: Google's `id_token`

Google returns a JWT as the `id_token`. If you use it (which avoids the `/userinfo` API call), you need to:

1. Fetch Google's JWKS endpoint (`https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs`)
2. Find the matching key by `kid` header
3. Decode the RSA public key from the JWK
4. Verify the JWT signature with that key
5. Validate claims: `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `email_verified`

Steps 3-4 require RSA public key parsing and signature verification. **Python's stdlib does not include this.** `cryptography` or `PyJWT` is what normally handles it.

**Your options:**

**Option A: Skip `id_token` entirely, call `/userinfo` instead.**
After token exchange, make a second HTTP call to `https://openidconnect.googleapis.com/v1/userinfo` with the `access_token`. You get the same email/sub data, no JWT validation needed. Authlib does this too when you don't explicitly parse the id_token. This is slightly less efficient (1 extra HTTP call on login) but **completely safe and stdlib-only**.

```python
# In your Google callback:
tokens = google_client.exchange_code(code, redirect_uri)
access_token = tokens["access_token"]
userinfo = google_client.get_userinfo(access_token)  # /userinfo endpoint

provider_user_id = userinfo["sub"]
email = userinfo["email"]
email_verified = userinfo.get("email_verified", False)
```

**Recommendation: use Option A.** Don't write a JWT validator by hand. The `/userinfo` endpoint is what Google explicitly provides for this pattern and it's what most simpler OAuth clients use.

---

## Complete route structure (stdlib only)

```python
# auth/oauth.py  — no Authlib, no Flask-Dance

from flask import Blueprint, redirect, request, session, url_for, current_app
from .oauth_client import OAuthClient, generate_state, verify_state
from .exceptions import OAuthError

oauth_bp = Blueprint("oauth", __name__)

def _get_client(provider: str) -> OAuthClient:
    cfg = current_app.config
    if provider == "google":
        return OAuthClient(
            client_id=cfg["GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID"],
            client_secret=cfg["GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET"],
            authorize_url="https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth",
            token_url="https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token",
            userinfo_url="https://openidconnect.googleapis.com/v1/userinfo",
            scope="openid email profile",
        )
    elif provider == "github":
        return OAuthClient(
            client_id=cfg["GITHUB_CLIENT_ID"],
            client_secret=cfg["GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET"],
            authorize_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize",
            token_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token",
            userinfo_url="https://api.github.com/user",
            scope="read:user user:email",
        )
    raise OAuthError(f"Unknown provider: {provider}")

@oauth_bp.route("/auth/login/<provider>")
def login(provider):
    client = _get_client(provider)
    state = generate_state()
    session["oauth_state"] = state
    session["oauth_next"] = request.args.get("next", "/")
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.callback", provider=provider, _external=True)
    return redirect(client.get_authorize_url(redirect_uri, state))

@oauth_bp.route("/auth/callback/<provider>")
def callback(provider):
    if not verify_state(session.pop("oauth_state", None), request.args.get("state")):
        raise OAuthError("State mismatch — possible CSRF")

    code = request.args.get("code")
    if not code:
        raise OAuthError("No code in callback")

    client = _get_client(provider)
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.callback", provider=provider, _external=True)

    try:
        tokens = client.exchange_code(code, redirect_uri)
    except Exception as e:
        raise OAuthError(f"Token exchange failed: {e}") from e

    if provider == "google":
        provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url = _parse_google(client, tokens)
    elif provider == "github":
        provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url = _parse_github(client, tokens)

    user, created = current_app.auth.find_or_create_oauth_user(
        provider=provider,
        provider_user_id=provider_user_id,
        email=email,
        display_name=display_name,
        avatar_url=avatar_url,
    )
    _set_user_session(user)

    next_url = session.pop("oauth_next", "/")
    return redirect(next_url)

def _parse_google(client, tokens):
    userinfo = client.get_userinfo(tokens["access_token"])
    if not userinfo.get("email_verified"):
        raise OAuthError("Google email not verified")
    return (
        userinfo["sub"],
        userinfo["email"],
        userinfo.get("name"),
        userinfo.get("picture"),
    )

def _parse_github(client, tokens):
    access_token = tokens["access_token"]
    profile = client.get_userinfo(access_token)  # /user
    email = _get_github_primary_email(access_token)
    return (
        str(profile["id"]),
        email,
        profile.get("name") or profile.get("login"),
        profile.get("avatar_url"),
    )

def _get_github_primary_email(access_token):
    import urllib.request, json
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        "https://api.github.com/user/emails",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
                 "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"},
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:
        emails = json.loads(resp.read())
    primary = next(
        (e["email"] for e in emails if e.get("primary") and e.get("verified")),
        None,
    )
    if not primary:
        raise OAuthError("No verified primary email on GitHub account")
    return primary
```

---

## What you lose vs. Authlib

| Feature | Authlib | DIY stdlib |
|---|---|---|
| CSRF / state param | Built-in | ✅ 5 lines with `secrets` + `hmac.compare_digest` |
| Token exchange | Built-in | ✅ ~15 lines urllib |
| Userinfo fetch | Built-in | ✅ ~10 lines urllib |
| Google `id_token` JWT validation | ✅ Full | ❌ Skipped — use `/userinfo` instead |
| PKCE | Built-in | ❌ Not implemented (not required for server-side flow) |
| Token refresh | Built-in | ❌ Not needed for login-only |
| Error handling nuance | Excellent | You own it |
| Provider config discovery (OIDC) | Built-in | ❌ Hardcode URLs (fine, they're stable) |

**Nothing in that "❌" column is actually needed for your use case.** PKCE is for public clients (SPAs, mobile apps). Token refresh is for persistent API access. OIDC discovery is nice but Google's URLs haven't changed in years. The `id_token` skip is the most meaningful trade — you make 1 extra HTTP call to `/userinfo` on login. That's it.

---

## Total line count

- `auth/oauth_client.py`: ~80 lines
- `auth/oauth.py` routes: ~100 lines
- Tests: ~120 lines (mock `urllib.request.urlopen` with `unittest.mock.patch`)

**~300 lines total.** Comparable to what you'd write with Authlib anyway — the library mostly saves you on the JWT handling, which you're not doing.

---

## My recommendation

**Do it.** The stdlib-only implementation is actually cleaner for your use case than the Authlib version because it removes abstraction you don't need. The one thing I'd say explicitly:

> **Don't use the `id_token` JWT directly. Always go to `/userinfo`.** The moment someone suggests "let's just decode the JWT without verifying the signature because it's faster," that's where it goes wrong. With this approach, that temptation doesn't exist.

One thing to resolve before you ship: the `_set_user_session` stub still needs the Flask-Login vs raw session answer. Everything else in this implementation is unblocked.

---

One more note for ops: when the next release cycle comes, dropping in Authlib is straightforward because your `_get_client` / `_parse_google` / `_parse_github` functions are the only things that change. The rest of `auth/oauth.py` and all of `auth/service.py` stay identical. The refactor path is clean.

strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and no test coverage. The 8K-line codebase has zero auth tests; only integration tests that log in with a hardcoded test user exist. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified and fixed: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation did not check associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still worked. Service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement. The security fix introduced 2 extra DB queries per API request, now collapsed to a single joined query.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Zero auth tests exist — only integration tests with a hardcoded test user
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed with a single joined query
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a confirmed product requirement

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content: Recommended: a single auth/ package. Structure: __init__.py (public API), models.py (User + credential models + OAuthIdentity), hashing.py (password hashing utils), tokens.py (API key + service account token validation), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three auth decorators), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy + OAuthError subclass), service.py (central AuthService class), oauth.py (OAuth routes + provider normalization), oauth_client.py (stdlib-only HTTP client). auth/service.py is the gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService, wired once in app factory via current_app.auth. OAuth2 adds identity acquisition (not resolution) — it feeds into existing resolve_session path once complete. auth/oauth.py is thin glue; all business logic is in AuthService. When Authlib is approved in a future release, only oauth_client.py + the _parse_* functions in oauth.py change — the rest of the auth package is unaffected.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
Wire AuthService once in app factory, access via current_app.auth
OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not identity resolution — it feeds into existing session path once complete
auth/oauth_client.py is a stdlib-only HTTP client — swap for Authlib next release by touching only oauth_client.py and _parse_* functions

strands.title: AuthService — Implemented Skeleton
strands.content: auth/service.py skeleton has been written in full. Three method groups: (1) Password: verify_password(email, raw_password) → User. Delegates to User.check_password_hash (bcrypt from existing models.py). Constant-time path even when user is None to prevent timing-based email enumeration. Checks is_active AFTER credential validation to avoid valid-email disclosure. (2) API key: resolve_api_key(raw_key) → (User|ServiceAccount, ApiKey). Single joined query with joinedload(ApiKey.user) + joinedload(ApiKey.service_account). Branches on api_key.service_account_id — human users get is_active check; service accounts bypass it. Synchronous last_used_at write via _touch_api_key (async later if profiling shows need). create_api_key returns (ApiKey, raw_key) — raw key returned once, never stored. Key hashing via SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are high-entropy, bcrypt would be a per-request performance disaster. (3) OAuth: find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) → (User, bool). Lookup order: OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id) → User by email (silent link) → create new User. flush() before OAuthIdentity creation to populate new_user.id as FK. Never updates User.email after creation (account takeover vector). link_oauth_identity and unlink_oauth_identity with lockout guard (has_password OR another provider). Cache layer deferred — join query alone may be sufficient.

key_claims.text: verify_password uses constant-time path even when user is None — prevents timing-based email enumeration
resolve_api_key uses a single joined query — no N+1
API key hashing uses SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are 256-bit entropy, fast hash is safe
find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: identity by provider_user_id → user by email → create new
flush() before OAuthIdentity creation is required to get new_user.id as FK — do not collapse to commit()
unlink_oauth_identity lockout guard: requires has_password=True OR another linked provider
Never auto-update User.email from provider data — account takeover vector

strands.title: OAuth2 — Stdlib Implementation
strands.content:
OAuth2 for Google + GitHub is implementable with zero new dependencies using stdlib urllib + secrets + hmac. The key insight: skip Google's id_token JWT validation (which requires RSA crypto not in stdlib) and use the /userinfo endpoint instead — 1 extra HTTP call on login, completely safe. What Authlib gives you that you actually need for server-side login: CSRF state (5 lines with secrets.token_urlsafe + hmac.compare_digest), token exchange (15 lines urllib), userinfo fetch (10 lines urllib). What you don't need: PKCE (public clients only), token refresh (login-only flow), OIDC discovery (URLs are stable, hardcode them), id_token JWT signature verification (replaced by /userinfo call).

Structure: auth/oauth_client.py (~80 lines) is a provider-agnostic OAuthClient class wrapping urllib. auth/oauth.py (~100 lines) contains routes + _parse_google/_parse_github. AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user is unchanged. Total: ~300 lines including tests.

Critical: never decode id_token without signature verification. With /userinfo approach, the temptation doesn't arise. GitHub requires /user/emails endpoint for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable (email may be private/null). State param stored in Flask session, verified with hmac.compare_digest to prevent timing attacks on CSRF check.

Upgrade path: when Authlib is approved next release, only oauth_client.py and the _parse_* functions in oauth.py change. auth/service.py, decorators, models — untouched. The seam is clean.

Mocking for tests: patch urllib.request.urlopen with unittest.mock.patch — no `responses` library needed (removes that dependency question).

key_claims.text: stdlib-only OAuth2 is viable for server-side login flows — no Authlib features are actually required
Google id_token JWT validation requires RSA crypto not in stdlib — skip it, use /userinfo instead
GitHub requires /user/emails call for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable
State CSRF protection: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) + hmac.compare_digest — 5 lines, no library needed
Total implementation is ~300 lines including tests
Upgrade path to Authlib next release touches only oauth_client.py and _parse_* functions
Test mocking: patch urllib.request.urlopen — no `responses` library needed

strands.title: Testing Strategy
strands.content:
Three-layer testing strategy for the auth/ package refactor. The constraint: don't test code being deleted, can't ship untested, don't want throwaway tests. The existing hardcoded-login integration tests serve as a regression net — they must pass after every PR.

Layer 1 — Unit tests for AuthService (tests/auth/test_service.py): Mock the DB session (MagicMock). No Flask, no HTTP, no real DB. Key security tests: verify_password wrong password raises InvalidCredentialsError; disabled account raises DisabledAccountError; timing-attack test asserts dummy hash is called even when user is None; resolve_api_key revoked key raises AuthError; service account bypasses is_active check; find_or_create_oauth_user never mutates User.email post-creation.

Layer 2 — Integration tests for decorators + routes (tests/auth/test_decorators.py, test_session_routes.py): Flask test client + in-memory SQLite + app factory.

Layer 3 — OAuth tests (tests/auth/test_oauth.py): Mock urllib.request.urlopen with unittest.mock.patch (no `responses` library needed — removes that external dep). Test: new user created on first OAuth login; existing user matched by identity; email collision links accounts; email_verified=False on Google returns error; GitHub no verified primary email returns error.

Security tests tagged pytest.mark.auth_security to prevent future removal. PR sequencing: exceptions → models → service (Layer 1) → decorators (Layer 2) → session routes (Layer 2) → oauth (Layer 3). Flask-Login question still blocks oauth.py merge.

Layer 3 OAuth tests mock urllib.request.urlopen — no `responses` library needed
Timing attack test must assert dummy hash is called even when user is None — not just that exception is raised
pytest.mark.auth_security marker should tag security-critical tests to prevent future removal

No new pip dependencies required for this release.

Most likely next questions: (a) Flask-Login answer — still the top blocker, resolves _set_user_session; (b) they may want to see the full test file for auth/oauth.py using unittest.mock.patch on urlopen — the fake JSON responses pattern is non-obvious; (c) they may ask about error handling in the urlopen calls (urllib raises HTTPError/URLError, not the clean exceptions Authlib raises — show wrapping pattern); (d) they may ask about timeout configuration for the urllib calls (hardcoded 10s — should be configurable); (e) they may ask about the upgrade path to Authlib next release more concretely. response_text:
Ouch. That's a real constraint, and the ops team is right to be cautious after a dep incident. The short answer is: **yes, you can implement OAuth2 without Authlib or Flask-Dance, but you're going to write a non-trivial amount of security-sensitive code by hand.** Let me be direct about the tradeoffs before you decide.

---

## What OAuth2 actually is, stripped down

For your use case (Google + GitHub login), the flow is:

1. **Authorization redirect** — build a URL with `client_id`, `redirect_uri`, `scope`, `state`, `response_type=code`. Redirect user there.
2. **Callback** — receive `code` + `state` from provider. Verify `state` matches what you sent.
3. **Token exchange** — POST to provider's token endpoint with `code`, get back `access_token` (and for Google: `id_token`).
4. **User info fetch** — call provider's API with `access_token` to get user profile.
5. **Your code** — call `find_or_create_oauth_user(...)`, set session.

The standard library covers all the HTTP mechanics. What a library like Authlib gives you beyond that:

- **PKCE** (code verifier/challenge generation)
- **JWT validation** of Google's `id_token` (signature verification against JWKS)
- **State param generation and CSRF protection** (trivial to replicate)
- **Provider config discovery** (OIDC discovery URL parsing)
- **Token refresh** (you don't need this for login-only)

The hard part you cannot safely skip: **Google's `id_token` JWT validation**.

---

## The real cost breakdown

### What's easy (stdlib only, ~50 lines total)

```python
# auth/oauth_client.py

import hashlib
import hmac
import json
import os
import secrets
import urllib.parse
import urllib.request

class OAuthClient:
    """Minimal OAuth2 client. No external dependencies."""

    def __init__(self, client_id, client_secret, authorize_url,
                 token_url, userinfo_url=None, scope=""):
        self.client_id = client_id
        self.client_secret = client_secret
        self.authorize_url = authorize_url
        self.token_url = token_url
        self.userinfo_url = userinfo_url
        self.scope = scope

    def get_authorize_url(self, redirect_uri, state):
        params = {
            "client_id": self.client_id,
            "redirect_uri": redirect_uri,
            "response_type": "code",
            "scope": self.scope,
            "state": state,
        }
        return self.authorize_url + "?" + urllib.parse.urlencode(params)

    def exchange_code(self, code, redirect_uri):
        data = urllib.parse.urlencode({
            "client_id": self.client_id,
            "client_secret": self.client_secret,
            "code": code,
            "redirect_uri": redirect_uri,
            "grant_type": "authorization_code",
        }).encode()
        req = urllib.request.Request(
            self.token_url,
            data=data,
            headers={"Accept": "application/json"},
            method="POST",
        )
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:
            return json.loads(resp.read())

    def get_userinfo(self, access_token):
        req = urllib.request.Request(
            self.userinfo_url,
            headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}"},
        )
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:
            return json.loads(resp.read())
```

State management (CSRF protection):

```python
def generate_state():
    return secrets.token_urlsafe(32)

def verify_state(session_state, callback_state):
    if not session_state or not callback_state:
        return False
    return hmac.compare_digest(session_state, callback_state)
```

**This part is fine.** It's just HTTP. ~80 lines total. Readable, testable, no magic.

---

### What's hard: Google's `id_token`

Google returns a JWT as the `id_token`. If you use it (which avoids the `/userinfo` API call), you need to:

1. Fetch Google's JWKS endpoint (`https://www.googleapis.com/oauth2/v3/certs`)
2. Find the matching key by `kid` header
3. Decode the RSA public key from the JWK
4. Verify the JWT signature with that key
5. Validate claims: `iss`, `aud`, `exp`, `email_verified`

Steps 3-4 require RSA public key parsing and signature verification. **Python's stdlib does not include this.** `cryptography` or `PyJWT` is what normally handles it.

**Your options:**

**Option A: Skip `id_token` entirely, call `/userinfo` instead.**
After token exchange, make a second HTTP call to `https://openidconnect.googleapis.com/v1/userinfo` with the `access_token`. You get the same email/sub data, no JWT validation needed. Authlib does this too when you don't explicitly parse the id_token. This is slightly less efficient (1 extra HTTP call on login) but **completely safe and stdlib-only**.

```python
# In your Google callback:
tokens = google_client.exchange_code(code, redirect_uri)
access_token = tokens["access_token"]
userinfo = google_client.get_userinfo(access_token)  # /userinfo endpoint

provider_user_id = userinfo["sub"]
email = userinfo["email"]
email_verified = userinfo.get("email_verified", False)
```

**Recommendation: use Option A.** Don't write a JWT validator by hand. The `/userinfo` endpoint is what Google explicitly provides for this pattern and it's what most simpler OAuth clients use.

---

## Complete route structure (stdlib only)

```python
# auth/oauth.py  — no Authlib, no Flask-Dance

from flask import Blueprint, redirect, request, session, url_for, current_app
from .oauth_client import OAuthClient, generate_state, verify_state
from .exceptions import OAuthError

oauth_bp = Blueprint("oauth", __name__)

def _get_client(provider: str) -> OAuthClient:
    cfg = current_app.config
    if provider == "google":
        return OAuthClient(
            client_id=cfg["GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID"],
            client_secret=cfg["GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET"],
            authorize_url="https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth",
            token_url="https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token",
            userinfo_url="https://openidconnect.googleapis.com/v1/userinfo",
            scope="openid email profile",
        )
    elif provider == "github":
        return OAuthClient(
            client_id=cfg["GITHUB_CLIENT_ID"],
            client_secret=cfg["GITHUB_CLIENT_SECRET"],
            authorize_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/authorize",
            token_url="https://github.com/login/oauth/access_token",
            userinfo_url="https://api.github.com/user",
            scope="read:user user:email",
        )
    raise OAuthError(f"Unknown provider: {provider}")

@oauth_bp.route("/auth/login/<provider>")
def login(provider):
    client = _get_client(provider)
    state = generate_state()
    session["oauth_state"] = state
    session["oauth_next"] = request.args.get("next", "/")
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.callback", provider=provider, _external=True)
    return redirect(client.get_authorize_url(redirect_uri, state))

@oauth_bp.route("/auth/callback/<provider>")
def callback(provider):
    if not verify_state(session.pop("oauth_state", None), request.args.get("state")):
        raise OAuthError("State mismatch — possible CSRF")

    code = request.args.get("code")
    if not code:
        raise OAuthError("No code in callback")

    client = _get_client(provider)
    redirect_uri = url_for("oauth.callback", provider=provider, _external=True)

    try:
        tokens = client.exchange_code(code, redirect_uri)
    except Exception as e:
        raise OAuthError(f"Token exchange failed: {e}") from e

    if provider == "google":
        provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url = _parse_google(client, tokens)
    elif provider == "github":
        provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url = _parse_github(client, tokens)

    user, created = current_app.auth.find_or_create_oauth_user(
        provider=provider,
        provider_user_id=provider_user_id,
        email=email,
        display_name=display_name,
        avatar_url=avatar_url,
    )
    _set_user_session(user)

    next_url = session.pop("oauth_next", "/")
    return redirect(next_url)

def _parse_google(client, tokens):
    userinfo = client.get_userinfo(tokens["access_token"])
    if not userinfo.get("email_verified"):
        raise OAuthError("Google email not verified")
    return (
        userinfo["sub"],
        userinfo["email"],
        userinfo.get("name"),
        userinfo.get("picture"),
    )

def _parse_github(client, tokens):
    access_token = tokens["access_token"]
    profile = client.get_userinfo(access_token)  # /user
    email = _get_github_primary_email(access_token)
    return (
        str(profile["id"]),
        email,
        profile.get("name") or profile.get("login"),
        profile.get("avatar_url"),
    )

def _get_github_primary_email(access_token):
    import urllib.request, json
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        "https://api.github.com/user/emails",
        headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
                 "Accept": "application/vnd.github+json"},
    )
    with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10) as resp:
        emails = json.loads(resp.read())
    primary = next(
        (e["email"] for e in emails if e.get("primary") and e.get("verified")),
        None,
    )
    if not primary:
        raise OAuthError("No verified primary email on GitHub account")
    return primary
```

---

## What you lose vs. Authlib

| Feature | Authlib | DIY stdlib |
|---|---|---|
| CSRF / state param | Built-in | ✅ 5 lines with `secrets` + `hmac.compare_digest` |
| Token exchange | Built-in | ✅ ~15 lines urllib |
| Userinfo fetch | Built-in | ✅ ~10 lines urllib |
| Google `id_token` JWT validation | ✅ Full | ❌ Skipped — use `/userinfo` instead |
| PKCE | Built-in | ❌ Not implemented (not required for server-side flow) |
| Token refresh | Built-in | ❌ Not needed for login-only |
| Error handling nuance | Excellent | You own it |
| Provider config discovery (OIDC) | Built-in | ❌ Hardcode URLs (fine, they're stable) |

**Nothing in that "❌" column is actually needed for your use case.** PKCE is for public clients (SPAs, mobile apps). Token refresh is for persistent API access. OIDC discovery is nice but Google's URLs haven't changed in years. The `id_token` skip is the most meaningful trade — you make 1 extra HTTP call to `/userinfo` on login. That's it.

---

## Total line count

- `auth/oauth_client.py`: ~80 lines
- `auth/oauth.py` routes: ~100 lines
- Tests: ~120 lines (mock `urllib.request.urlopen` with `unittest.mock.patch`)

**~300 lines total.** Comparable to what you'd write with Authlib anyway — the library mostly saves you on the JWT handling, which you're not doing.

---

## My recommendation

**Do it.** The stdlib-only implementation is actually cleaner for your use case than the Authlib version because it removes abstraction you don't need. The one thing I'd say explicitly:

> **Don't use the `id_token` JWT directly. Always go to `/userinfo`.** The moment someone suggests "let's just decode the JWT without verifying the signature because it's faster," that's where it goes wrong. With this approach, that temptation doesn't exist.

One thing to resolve before you ship: the `_set_user_session` stub still needs the Flask-Login vs raw session answer. Everything else in this implementation is unblocked.

---

One more note for ops: when the next release cycle comes, dropping in Authlib is straightforward because your `_get_client` / `_parse_google` / `_parse_github` functions are the only things that change. The rest of `auth/oauth.py` and all of `auth/service.py` stay identical. The refactor path is clean.

strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and no test coverage. The 8K-line codebase has zero auth tests; only integration tests that log in with a hardcoded test user exist. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified and fixed: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation did not check associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still worked. Service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement. The security fix introduced 2 extra DB queries per API request, now collapsed to a single joined query.

Zero auth tests exist — only integration tests with a hardcoded test user
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Service accounts use the same API key path as human users but have no is_active flag
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed with a single joined query
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a confirmed product requirement

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content: Recommended: a single auth/ package. Structure: __init__.py (public API), models.py (User + credential models + OAuthIdentity), hashing.py (password hashing utils), tokens.py (API key + service account token validation), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three auth decorators), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy + OAuthError subclass), service.py (central AuthService class), oauth.py (OAuth routes + provider normalization), oauth_client.py (stdlib-only HTTP client). auth/service.py is the gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService, wired once in app factory via current_app.auth. OAuth2 adds identity acquisition (not resolution) — it feeds into existing resolve_session path once complete. auth/oauth.py is thin glue; all business logic is in AuthService. When Authlib is approved in a future release, only oauth_client.py + the _parse_* functions in oauth.py change — the rest of the auth package is unaffected.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
Wire AuthService once in app factory, access via current_app.auth
OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not identity resolution — it feeds into existing session path once complete
auth/oauth_client.py is a stdlib-only HTTP client — swap for Authlib next release by touching only oauth_client.py and _parse_* functions

strands.title: AuthService — Implemented Skeleton
strands.content: auth/service.py skeleton has been written in full. Three method groups: (1) Password: verify_password(email, raw_password) → User. Delegates to User.check_password_hash (bcrypt from existing models.py). Constant-time path even when user is None to prevent timing-based email enumeration. Checks is_active AFTER credential validation to avoid valid-email disclosure. (2) API key: resolve_api_key(raw_key) → (User|ServiceAccount, ApiKey). Single joined query with joinedload(ApiKey.user) + joinedload(ApiKey.service_account). Branches on api_key.service_account_id — human users get is_active check; service accounts bypass it. Synchronous last_used_at write via _touch_api_key (async later if profiling shows need). create_api_key returns (ApiKey, raw_key) — raw key returned once, never stored. Key hashing via SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are high-entropy, bcrypt would be a per-request performance disaster. (3) OAuth: find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) → (User, bool). Lookup order: OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id) → User by email (silent link) → create new User. flush() before OAuthIdentity creation to populate new_user.id as FK. Never updates User.email after creation (account takeover vector). link_oauth_identity and unlink_oauth_identity with lockout guard (has_password OR another provider). Cache layer deferred — join query alone may be sufficient.

key_claims.text: verify_password uses constant-time path even when user is None — prevents timing-based email enumeration
resolve_api_key uses a single joined query — no N+1
API key hashing uses SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are 256-bit entropy, fast hash is safe
find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: identity by provider_user_id → user by email → create new
flush() before OAuthIdentity creation is required to get new_user.id as FK — do not collapse to commit()
unlink_oauth_identity lockout guard: requires has_password=True OR another linked provider
Never auto-update User.email from provider data — account takeover vector

strands.title: OAuth2 — Stdlib Implementation
strands.content:
OAuth2 for Google + GitHub is implementable with zero new dependencies using stdlib urllib + secrets + hmac. The key insight: skip Google's id_token JWT validation (which requires RSA crypto not in stdlib) and use the /userinfo endpoint instead — 1 extra HTTP call on login, completely safe. What Authlib gives you that you actually need for server-side login: CSRF state (5 lines with secrets.token_urlsafe + hmac.compare_digest), token exchange (15 lines urllib), userinfo fetch (10 lines urllib). What you don't need: PKCE (public clients only), token refresh (login-only flow), OIDC discovery (URLs are stable, hardcode them), id_token JWT signature verification (replaced by /userinfo call).

Structure: auth/oauth_client.py (~80 lines) is a provider-agnostic OAuthClient class wrapping urllib. auth/oauth.py (~100 lines) contains routes + _parse_google/_parse_github. AuthService.find_or_create_oauth_user is unchanged. Total: ~300 lines including tests.

Critical: never decode id_token without signature verification. With /userinfo approach, the temptation doesn't arise. GitHub requires /user/emails endpoint for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable (email may be private/null). State param stored in Flask session, verified with hmac.compare_digest to prevent timing attacks on CSRF check.

Upgrade path: when Authlib is approved next release, only oauth_client.py and the _parse_* functions in oauth.py change. auth/service.py, decorators, models — untouched. The seam is clean.

Mocking for tests: patch urllib.request.urlopen with unittest.mock.patch — no `responses` library needed (removes that dependency question).

key_claims.text: stdlib-only OAuth2 is viable for server-side login flows — no Authlib features are actually required
Google id_token JWT validation requires RSA crypto not in stdlib — skip it, use /userinfo instead
GitHub requires /user/emails call for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable
State CSRF protection: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) + hmac.compare_digest — 5 lines, no library needed
Total implementation is ~300 lines including tests
Upgrade path to Authlib next release touches only oauth_client.py and _parse_* functions
Test mocking: patch urllib.request.urlopen — no `responses` library needed

strands.title: Testing Strategy
strands.content:
Three-layer testing strategy for the auth/ package refactor. The constraint: don't test code being deleted, can't ship untested, don't want throwaway tests. The existing hardcoded-login integration tests serve as a regression net — they must pass after every PR.

Layer 1 — Unit tests for AuthService (tests/auth/test_service.py): Mock the DB session (MagicMock). No Flask, no HTTP, no real DB. Key security tests: verify_password wrong password raises InvalidCredentialsError; disabled account raises DisabledAccountError; timing-attack test asserts dummy hash is called even when user is None; resolve_api_key revoked key raises AuthError; service account bypasses is_active check; find_or_create_oauth_user never mutates User.email post-creation.

Layer 2 — Integration tests for decorators + routes (tests/auth/test_decorators.py, test_session_routes.py): Flask test client + in-memory SQLite + app factory.

Layer 3 — OAuth tests (tests/auth/test_oauth.py): Mock urllib.request.urlopen with unittest.mock.patch (no `responses` library needed — removes that external dep). Test: new user created on first OAuth login; existing user matched by identity; email collision links accounts; email_verified=False on Google returns error; GitHub no verified primary email returns error.

Security tests tagged pytest.mark.auth_security to prevent future removal. PR sequencing: exceptions → models → service (Layer 1) → decorators (Layer 2) → session routes (Layer 2) → oauth (Layer 3). Flask-Login question still blocks oauth.py merge.

key_claims.text: Existing integration tests are a regression net, not a test suite — must pass after every PR
Layer 3 OAuth tests mock urllib.request.urlopen — no `responses` library needed
Timing attack test must assert dummy hash is called even when user is None — not just that exception is raised
pytest.mark.auth_security marker should tag security-critical tests to prevent future removal

strands.title: Implementation Plan
strands.content: Ordered migration steps with current status: (0) DONE: security patches applied. (0b) DONE IN SKELETON: resolve_api_key join query; last_used_at async deferred. (0c) DONE IN SKELETON: OAuthIdentity model + find_or_create_oauth_user + oauth routes. Full refactor sequence: (1) auth/exceptions.py — no tests needed, unblocks everything; (2) auth/models.py (OAuthIdentity, has_password on User) + migration; (3) auth/oauth_client.py (stdlib HTTP client, ~80 lines); (4) auth/service.py + tests/auth/test_service.py (Layer 1); (5) auth/decorators.py + tests/auth/test_decorators.py; (6) auth/hashing.py; (7) auth/session.py + tests/auth/test_session_routes.py; (8) auth/oauth.py + tests/auth/test_oauth.py (Flask-Login Q must be resolved first); (9) auth/__init__.py; (10) cleanup call sites. No new pip dependencies required for this release.

key_claims.text: No new pip dependencies required — stdlib-only OAuth is viable for this release
Flask-Login question is a merge blocker for auth/oauth.py
exceptions.py must come first — unblocks all other extractions
Tests are written per-PR in lockstep with implementation, not after

strands.title: Unknown Unknowns
strands.content: Remaining unknowns: (1) Flask-Login vs custom session management — PR-blocking for oauth.py; determines _set_user_session implementation. (2) Service account schema — same table vs separate, discriminator vs nullable FK; determines resolve_api_key branch logic. (3) Redis in stack — determines cache backend if profiling shows cache is needed. (4) Compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation — determines whether explicit cache invalidation is required. (5) Email collision policy preference — silent link (current default) vs explicit confirmation. (6) has_password column migration — existing users need default=True backfill. (7) Test infrastructure: pytest + SQLite or heavier? App factory exists or module-level construction?

key_claims.text: Flask-Login vs custom sessions is PR-blocking for oauth.py
Existing users need has_password=True backfill in the migration — do not default to False
Whether a create_app factory exists determines Layer 2 test fixture complexity

open_questions: Are you using Flask-Login (LoginManager) or raw session management? This is a merge blocker for auth/oauth.py — determines whether _set_user_session uses login_user(user) or session['user_id'] = user.id.
What is the ApiKey/ServiceAccount schema? (discriminator column, nullable FKs, or separate tables) — determines the branching logic in resolve_api_key.
Do you have compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation on user disable? Determines whether cache TTL is acceptable or explicit invalidation is required.
Email collision policy: silent linking (current default in skeleton) or explicit user confirmation screen when an OAuth email matches an existing password account?
Does your User model already have a has_password field, or does the migration need to add it with a default=True backfill for existing users?
Test infrastructure: pytest + SQLite or Postgres? Does a create_app factory already exist or is the app constructed at module level?
tensor.instructions_for_next: Stdlib OAuth implementation is now the plan. Most likely next questions: (a) Flask-Login answer — still the top blocker, resolves _set_user_session; (b) they may want to see the full test file for auth/oauth.py using unittest.mock.patch on urlopen — the fake JSON responses pattern is non-obvious; (c) they may ask about error handling in the urlopen calls (urllib raises HTTPError/URLError, not the clean exceptions Authlib raises — show wrapping pattern); (d) they may ask about timeout configuration for the urllib calls (hardcoded 10s — should be configurable); (e) they may ask about the upgrade path to Authlib next release more concretely. Watch for: if they're already on Python 3.11+ they have tomllib but nothing OAuth-relevant in stdlib. The urllib approach has one rough edge: urlopen raises urllib.error.HTTPError on 4xx/5xx — need to wrap in try/except and convert to OAuthError in exchange_code and get_userinfo. This wasn't shown explicitly in the code above and should be added if asked. Also: ops team may ask about vendoring Authlib instead of pip install — that's a reasonable ask and worth mentioning proactively if they push back on the stdlib approach.

user_message: Let's step back. We started by saying we'd consolidate all auth into `auth/service.py`. But now we have three auth flows, a caching layer, the security fixes, and zero tests. Are we over-engineering this? Would it be simpler to just fix the security bugs and add OAuth2 as a separate module without the big refactor?

strands.title: Testing Strategy
strands.content:
Three-layer testing strategy for the auth/ package refactor. The constraint: don't test code being deleted, can't ship untested, don't want throwaway tests. The existing hardcoded-login integration tests serve as a regression net — they must pass after every PR.

Layer 1 — Unit tests for AuthService (tests/auth/test_service.py): Mock the DB session (MagicMock). No Flask, no HTTP, no real DB. Key security tests: verify_password wrong password raises InvalidCredentialsError; disabled account raises DisabledAccountError; timing-attack test asserts dummy hash is called even when user is None; resolve_api_key revoked key raises AuthError; service account bypasses is_active check; find_or_create_oauth_user never mutates User.email post-creation.

Layer 2 — Integration tests for decorators + routes (tests/auth/test_decorators.py, test_session_routes.py): Flask test client + in-memory SQLite + app factory.

Layer 3 — OAuth tests (tests/auth/test_oauth.py): Mock urllib.request.urlopen with unittest.mock.patch (no `responses` library needed — removes that external dep). Test: new user created on first OAuth login; existing user matched by identity; email collision links accounts; email_verified=False on Google returns error; GitHub no verified primary email returns error.

Security tests tagged pytest.mark.auth_security to prevent future removal. PR sequencing: exceptions → models → service (Layer 1) → decorators (Layer 2) → session routes (Layer 2) → oauth (Layer 3). Flask-Login question still blocks oauth.py merge.

key_claims.text: Existing integration tests are a regression net, not a test suite — must pass after every PR
Layer 3 OAuth tests mock urllib.request.urlopen — no `responses` library needed
Timing attack test must assert dummy hash is called even when user is None — not just that exception is raised
pytest.mark.auth_security marker should tag security-critical tests to prevent future removal

strands.title: Implementation Plan
strands.content: Ordered migration steps with current status: (0) DONE: security patches applied. (0b) DONE IN SKELETON: resolve_api_key join query; last_used_at async deferred. (0c) DONE IN SKELETON: OAuthIdentity model + find_or_create_oauth_user + oauth routes. Full refactor sequence: (1) auth/exceptions.py — no tests needed, unblocks everything; (2) auth/models.py (OAuthIdentity, has_password on User) + migration; (3) auth/oauth_client.py (stdlib HTTP client, ~80 lines); (4) auth/service.py + tests/auth/test_service.py (Layer 1); (5) auth/decorators.py + tests/auth/test_decorators.py; (6) auth/hashing.py; (7) auth/session.py + tests/auth/test_session_routes.py; (8) auth/oauth.py + tests/auth/test_oauth.py (Flask-Login Q must be resolved first); (9) auth/__init__.py; (10) cleanup call sites. No new pip dependencies required for this release.

key_claims.text: No new pip dependencies required — stdlib-only OAuth is viable for this release
Flask-Login question is a merge blocker for auth/oauth.py
exceptions.py must come first — unblocks all other extractions
Tests are written per-PR in lockstep with implementation, not after

strands.title: Unknown Unknowns
strands.content: Remaining unknowns: (1) Flask-Login vs custom session management — PR-blocking for oauth.py; determines _set_user_session implementation. (2) Service account schema — same table vs separate, discriminator vs nullable FK; determines resolve_api_key branch logic. (3) Redis in stack — determines cache backend if profiling shows cache is needed. (4) Compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation — determines whether explicit cache invalidation is required. (5) Email collision policy preference — silent link (current default) vs explicit confirmation. (6) has_password column migration — existing users need default=True backfill. (7) Test infrastructure: pytest + SQLite or heavier? App factory exists or module-level construction?

key_claims.text: Flask-Login vs custom sessions is PR-blocking for oauth.py
Existing users need has_password=True backfill in the migration — do not default to False
Whether a create_app factory exists determines Layer 2 test fixture complexity

open_questions: Are you using Flask-Login (LoginManager) or raw session management? This is a merge blocker for auth/oauth.py — determines whether _set_user_session uses login_user(user) or session['user_id'] = user.id.
What is the ApiKey/ServiceAccount schema? (discriminator column, nullable FKs, or separate tables) — determines the branching logic in resolve_api_key.
Do you have compliance/audit requirements for immediate revocation on user disable? Determines whether cache TTL is acceptable or explicit invalidation is required.
Email collision policy: silent linking (current default in skeleton) or explicit user confirmation screen when an OAuth email matches an existing password account?
Does your User model already have a has_password field, or does the migration need to add it with a default=True backfill for existing users?
Test infrastructure: pytest + SQLite or Postgres? Does a create_app factory already exist or is the app constructed at module level?
prior_tensor.instructions_for_next: Stdlib OAuth implementation is now the plan. Most likely next questions: (a) Flask-Login answer — still the top blocker, resolves _set_user_session; (b) they may want to see the full test file for auth/oauth.py using unittest.mock.patch on urlopen — the fake JSON responses pattern is non-obvious; (c) they may ask about error handling in the urlopen calls (urllib raises HTTPError/URLError, not the clean exceptions Authlib raises — show wrapping pattern); (d) they may ask about timeout configuration for the urllib calls (hardcoded 10s — should be configurable); (e) they may ask about the upgrade path to Authlib next release more concretely. Watch for: if they're already on Python 3.11+ they have tomllib but nothing OAuth-relevant in stdlib. The urllib approach has one rough edge: urlopen raises urllib.error.HTTPError on 4xx/5xx — need to wrap in try/except and convert to OAuthError in exchange_code and get_userinfo. This wasn't shown explicitly in the code above and should be added if asked. Also: ops team may ask about vendoring Authlib instead of pip install — that's a reasonable ask and worth mentioning proactively if they push back on the stdlib approach.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: auth/oauth.py Authlib-specific implementation details: init_oauth(app), Authlib OIDC discovery URL registration, Authlib token parsing via id_token, Authlib state param mechanics
declared_losses.why: Entire Authlib approach is superseded by stdlib-only implementation. The Authlib skeleton is no longer the implementation plan — it's the future upgrade target.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: OAuth2 Integration — Design Decisions strand (standalone)
declared_losses.why: Content fully absorbed into OAuth2 — Stdlib Implementation and AuthService — Implemented Skeleton. The design decisions (OAuthIdentity model, has_password, email collision, CSRF, lockout guard) are all still present in those strands. Consolidating per harness feedback to reduce strand count.

raw_output.response:
Honest answer: yes, you might be over-engineering it. Let me actually think through the tradeoff.

**The case for "just fix the bugs + bolt-on OAuth":**

- Two security bugs are fixed. Ship them. Done.
- OAuth2 as `auth/oauth.py` that imports directly from `models.py` and `decorators.py` — maybe 150 lines, no new abstractions.
- Total scope: ~200 lines, 1 PR, done this week.
- The 8K-line codebase stays as-is. Tests cover the new code only.

This is legitimate. It's not cowardice — it's recognizing that a refactor that never ships is worse than working messy code.

**Where it breaks down:**

The question is whether the *current* scattered auth state actively causes bugs or blocks work, beyond the two you've already fixed. Here's the honest assessment:

1. **The bugs you fixed weren't accidents** — they happened because there are 3 separate auth paths with no shared "is this identity valid?" check. If you bolt OAuth onto that same foundation, you're adding a 4th path with the same structural gap. You'll ship OAuth, then discover that OAuth users with disabled accounts still work, because that check lives in 3 places instead of 4.

2. **AuthService isn't about architecture beauty** — it's about having one place that asks "is this identity currently valid?" so the next disabled-account bug is impossible, not just fixed. That's a real risk reduction, not gold-plating.

3. **The "big refactor" framing is wrong** — I've been presenting this as a grand restructuring, but what we actually have is:
   - `auth/exceptions.py` — 30 lines, no risk
   - `auth/service.py` — mostly code that already exists, moved + unified
   - `auth/oauth.py` + `auth/oauth_client.py` — new code regardless of which approach you take
   
   The models change (OAuthIdentity) is required *either way*. The decorator change is small. The actual incremental work of doing it "right" versus bolting on is maybe 2-3 days.

**The real question is: what's your timeline?**

If you need OAuth in production in 3 days, bolt it on. Explicitly. Leave a `# TODO: consolidate into auth/service.py` comment, keep the PR small, come back later. That's a rational tradeoff.

If you have a week, the refactor is worth it — not because it's elegant, but because you're adding OAuth right now, which means you're already touching auth code. Moving code while you're already reading it costs much less than moving it cold later.

**My actual recommendation:**

Do the minimum viable refactor, not the full one. That means:

1. **Keep:** `auth/exceptions.py` (30 lines, unblocks clean error handling everywhere)
2. **Keep:** `auth/service.py` but *only* the methods you're shipping now — `verify_password`, `resolve_api_key`, `find_or_create_oauth_user`. No cache layer. No `link_oauth_identity`/`unlink_oauth_identity` until someone asks for it.
3. **Keep:** `auth/oauth_client.py` + `auth/oauth.py` (you need this regardless)
4. **Drop:** Everything speculative — the cache layer, the async `last_used_at`, the `unlink_oauth_identity` lockout guard, the `has_password` migration (defer until someone requests OAuth unlinking).

That cuts the scope roughly in half. You get the structural safety benefit (one valid-identity check) without the speculative features. The tests are smaller. The PR is reviewable.

**What I'd explicitly not do:**

Don't add the cache layer until you have a measured performance problem. Don't add `link_oauth_identity` until a user can trigger it from the UI. Don't add `unlink_oauth_identity` until `link_oauth_identity` exists.

So: not over-engineered in intent, but over-scoped in execution. The auth/ package is worth doing. The speculative features aren't.

strands.title: Problem Diagnosis
strands.content: Auth logic is scattered across at least 4 files: app.py (login/logout routes), models.py (User model + password hashing), decorators.py (@login_required), api/routes.py (API key validation), jobs/worker.py (service account tokens). Three distinct auth mechanisms exist — session, API key, service account — with no shared error handling, no unified identity resolution, and no test coverage. The 8K-line codebase has zero auth tests; only integration tests that log in with a hardcoded test user exist. Two confirmed security bugs have been identified and fixed: (1) @login_required caches g.user but doesn't check account-disabled status; (2) API key validation did not check associated user's account status — a disabled user's keys still worked. Service accounts use the same API key infrastructure as human users but have no is_active flag. New scope: OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) has been added as a product requirement. The security fix introduced 2 extra DB queries per API request, now collapsed to a single joined query.

key_claims.text: Auth is scattered across 4+ files with no shared abstractions
Zero auth tests exist — only integration tests with a hardcoded test user
Three distinct mechanisms exist: session, API key, service account
Bug: API key validation did not check user account status — now fixed with a single joined query
OAuth2 (Google + GitHub) is a confirmed product requirement

strands.title: Scope Calibration
strands.content:
The refactor is not over-engineered in intent, but was over-scoped in execution. AuthService's value is structural safety (one place that asks 'is this identity currently valid?'), not architectural beauty. The scattered-auth bug pattern is not accidental — it's structural: 3 separate auth paths with no shared validity check means any new path (OAuth) will repeat the same disabled-account class of bug. That's a real risk reduction, not gold-plating.

But the plan accumulated speculative features: async last_used_at, cache layer, link_oauth_identity/unlink_oauth_identity, has_password migration. None of these are required to ship OAuth safely. They should be dropped until someone triggers the need.

The minimum viable refactor: auth/exceptions.py (30 lines), auth/service.py (verify_password + resolve_api_key + find_or_create_oauth_user only), auth/oauth_client.py + auth/oauth.py (required regardless of approach). Everything else deferred. This cuts scope roughly in half while preserving the structural safety benefit.

The bolt-on alternative (OAuth as standalone module, no service.py) is also rational if timeline is 3 days. It means explicitly accepting the risk of a 4th disconnected auth path. If chosen: leave a TODO comment, keep the PR small, revisit when the 4th disabled-account bug appears.

Timeline drives the choice: 3 days → bolt-on with explicit TODO. 1 week → minimum viable refactor.

key_claims.text: The bugs were structural, not accidental — bolting OAuth onto the same foundation creates a 4th disconnected auth path with the same class of vulnerability
Speculative features (cache, async last_used_at, link/unlink OAuth, has_password) should be dropped from current scope
Minimum viable refactor is ~half the original scope and still provides the structural safety benefit
Bolt-on is rational if timeline is 3 days — but must be explicit, not accidental

strands.title: Target Architecture
strands.content:
auth/ package, minimum viable scope. Structure: __init__.py (public API), exceptions.py (AuthError hierarchy), models.py (OAuthIdentity — required for OAuth regardless of approach), service.py (AuthService: verify_password, resolve_api_key, find_or_create_oauth_user only), oauth_client.py (stdlib HTTP client), oauth.py (routes + _parse_google/_parse_github), session.py (login/logout routes), decorators.py (all three decorators), hashing.py (password hashing utils). Dropped from scope: cache layer, async last_used_at, link_oauth_identity, unlink_oauth_identity, has_password column (defer until OAuth unlinking is a UI feature).

auth/service.py is the gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService, wired once in app factory via current_app.auth. OAuth2 is identity acquisition, not resolution — it feeds into existing resolve_session path once complete. When Authlib is approved next release, only oauth_client.py + _parse_* functions change.

key_claims.text: auth/service.py is the correct gravitational center — all identity resolution goes through AuthService
OAuthIdentity model is required regardless of approach — it belongs in auth/models.py
has_password column, link/unlink OAuth, cache layer deferred until triggered by actual requirements

strands.title: AuthService — Core Methods Only
strands.content:
auth/service.py ships three method groups only. (1) verify_password(email, raw_password) → User. Constant-time path even when user is None (dummy hash call) to prevent timing-based email enumeration. Checks is_active AFTER credential validation to avoid valid-email disclosure. (2) resolve_api_key(raw_key) → (User|ServiceAccount, ApiKey). Single joined query. Human users get is_active check; service accounts bypass it. Synchronous last_used_at write — async deferred until profiling shows need. create_api_key returns (ApiKey, raw_key) once, never stored. API key hashing: SHA-256 not bcrypt (keys are high-entropy; bcrypt per-request is a performance disaster). (3) find_or_create_oauth_user(provider, provider_user_id, email, display_name, avatar_url) → (User, bool). Lookup order: OAuthIdentity by (provider, provider_user_id) → User by email (silent link) → create new. flush() before OAuthIdentity creation to populate new_user.id as FK. Never updates User.email after creation (account takeover vector).

Dropped from service.py this release: link_oauth_identity, unlink_oauth_identity, cache layer, async last_used_at.

key_claims.text: verify_password uses constant-time path even when user is None — prevents timing-based email enumeration
resolve_api_key uses a single joined query — no N+1
API key hashing uses SHA-256 not bcrypt — keys are 256-bit entropy, fast hash is safe
find_or_create_oauth_user lookup order: identity by provider_user_id → user by email → create new
Never auto-update User.email from provider data — account takeover vector

strands.title: OAuth2 + Testing
strands.content:
OAuth2 for Google + GitHub is implementable with stdlib only (urllib + secrets + hmac). Key insight: skip Google id_token JWT validation (requires RSA crypto not in stdlib), use /userinfo endpoint instead — 1 extra HTTP call on login, completely safe. GitHub requires /user/emails for verified primary email — /user alone is unreliable. State CSRF: secrets.token_urlsafe(32) + hmac.compare_digest, stored in Flask session. Total: ~300 lines including tests. Upgrade path to Authlib next release touches only oauth_client.py and _parse_* functions.

Three-layer tests, written per-PR in lockstep. Layer 1 — Unit (test_service.py): mock DB session, no Flask. Key tests: wrong password raises error; timing test asserts dummy hash called even when user is None; disabled account raises error; service account bypasses is_active; find_or_create never mutates User.email. Layer 2 — Integration (test_decorators.py, test_session_routes.py): Flask test client + in-memory SQLite. Layer 3 — OAuth (test_oauth.py): mock urllib.request.urlopen with unittest.mock.patch — no `responses` library needed. Test: new user on first OAuth login; existing user matched by identity; email collision links accounts; email_verified=False returns error; GitHub no verified email returns error. Security tests tagged pytest.mark.auth_security.

Existing hardcoded-login integration tests serve as regression net — must pass after every PR.

key_claims.text: stdlib-only OAuth2 is viable — no Authlib features are actually required for server-side login
GitHub requires /user/emails call for verified primary email
Timing attack test must assert dummy hash is called even when user is None
Test mocking: patch urllib.request.urlopen — no `responses` library needed

strands.title: Implementation Plan
strands.content: Ordered migration steps. Security patches are done. PR sequence: (1) auth/exceptions.py — 30 lines, no tests needed, unblocks everything; (2) auth/models.py (OAuthIdentity only — no has_password yet) + migration; (3) auth/oauth_client.py; (4) auth/service.py + tests/auth/test_service.py; (5) auth/decorators.py + tests/auth/test_decorators.py; (6) auth/hashing.py; (7) auth/session.py + tests/auth/test_session_routes.py; (8) auth/oauth.py + tests/auth/test_oauth.py (Flask-Login Q must be resolved first); (9) auth/__init__.py; (10) cleanup call sites. No new pip dependencies. Flask-Login question is still a merge blocker for step 8.

key_claims.text: No new pip dependencies required — stdlib-only OAuth is viable for this release
Flask-Login question is a merge blocker for auth/oauth.py
